# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Conceptual / Short Answer)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define profile levelling. Mention its two main applications in civil engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
Profile levelling is the process of determining the relative elevations of a series of points at known horizontal distances along a defined line (such as the centre line of a road, railway, canal, or pipeline) and plotting them to obtain the longitudinal section of the ground surface.

**Two main applications:**
1. Fixing the longitudinal gradient and formation level of a proposed road / railway / canal.
2. Computing the volume of earthwork between successive cross-sections along the alignment.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Intermediate Sights (IS) and Foresights (FS) in levelling.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Intermediate Sight (IS):** A staff reading taken on a point *between* two Foresights within the *same* instrument setup, whose RL is required but which is *not* a Change Point. IS readings are used to fix elevations of intermediate points but do not establish a new HI.
- **Foresight (FS):** A staff reading taken on a Change Point at the *end* of an instrument setup, which is used to transfer the elevation to the next instrument position. The RL of the CP becomes the basis for computing the next HI.

| **Basis** | **IS** | **FS** |
|:----------|:-------|:-------|
| Position in setup | Between BS and FS | Last reading in the setup |
| Number per setup | Many | Exactly one (or zero if BM closes the run) |
| Purpose | RL of intermediate stations | Establishes CP for next HI |
| Affects HI? | No | Yes (becomes the basis for next HI) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** The following consecutive staff readings were taken during a profile levelling survey along a proposed road. The first reading was on a BM of RL 150.000 m and the last was on a BM of RL 149.820 m. Reduce the levels using the **Height of Instrument (HI) method** and apply the **arithmetic check**.

| **Station** | **BS** | **IS** | **FS** |
|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| 1 | 2.150 | | |
| 2 | | 1.625 | |
| 3 | | 1.945 | |
| 4 | | 2.310 | |
| 5 | 1.785 | | 1.105 |
| 6 | | 1.460 | |
| 7 | 0.945 | | 2.250 |
| 8 | | 1.880 | |
| 9 | | | 1.745 |

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the reduced levels obtained in part (a), identify the highest and lowest points, compute the **gradient** between them, and comment on whether the gradient is suitable for a *metalled road* (ruling gradient 1 in 30).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute HI of first setup.
$$
\text{HI}_1 = 150.000 + 2.150 = 152.150 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Reduce readings of first setup.

| **Station** | **BS** | **IS** | **FS** | **HI** | **RL (m)** |
|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 2.150 | | | 152.150 | 150.000 |
| 2 | | 1.625 | | 152.150 | 150.525 |
| 3 | | 1.945 | | 152.150 | 150.205 |
| 4 | | 2.310 | | 152.150 | 149.840 |
| 5 | 1.785 | | 1.105 | — | 151.045 |

> Detail of computation:
> - Station 1: BS → RL = 150.000 (BM given).
> - Station 2: IS → RL = 152.150 − 1.625 = 150.525 m.
> - Station 3: IS → RL = 152.150 − 1.945 = 150.205 m.
> - Station 4: IS → RL = 152.150 − 2.310 = 149.840 m.
> - Station 5: FS → RL of CP = 152.150 − 1.105 = 151.045 m.

**Step 3:** Compute HI of second setup (after shifting from station 5).
$$
\text{HI}_2 = 151.045 + 1.785 = 152.830 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** Reduce readings of second setup.

| **Station** | **BS** | **IS** | **FS** | **HI** | **RL (m)** |
|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:----------:|
| 6 | | 1.460 | | 152.830 | 151.370 |

**Step 5:** Compute HI of third setup.
$$
\text{HI}_3 = 151.370 + 0.945 = 152.315 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6:** Reduce readings of third setup.

| **Station** | **BS** | **IS** | **FS** | **HI** | **RL (m)** |
|:-----------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|:----------:|
| 7 | 0.945 | | 2.250 | — | 150.580 |
| 8 | | 1.880 | | 152.315 | 150.435 |
| 9 | | | 1.745 | — | 150.570 |

Detail:
- Station 7: FS → RL of CP = 152.830 − 2.250 = 150.580 m. BS = 0.945 m on same CP.
- Station 8: IS → RL = 152.315 − 1.880 = 150.435 m.
- Station 9: FS → RL = 152.315 − 1.745 = 150.570 m.

**Step 7:** Arithmetic Check.

$$
\Sigma \text{BS} = 2.150 + 1.785 + 0.945 = 4.880 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Sigma \text{FS} = 1.105 + 2.250 + 1.745 = 5.100 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Sigma \text{BS} - \Sigma \text{FS} = 4.880 - 5.100 = -0.220 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{Last RL} - \text{First RL} = 150.570 - 150.000 = 0.570 \text{ m}
$$

**Closure error:**
$$
e = 0.570 - (-0.220) = 0.790 \text{ m}
$$

Wait — let me re-check: the formula is
$$
\Sigma \text{BS} - \Sigma \text{FS} = \text{Last RL} - \text{First RL}
$$

The first RL is 150.000 m (BM, given). The last RL is 150.570 m (closing BM, computed). However, the *actual* closing BM is 149.820 m. Therefore:

$$
\text{Computed last RL} - \text{Actual closing BM} = 150.570 - 149.820 = 0.750 \text{ m (closing error)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the closing BM check. The arithmetic $\Sigma BS - \Sigma FS = \text{Last RL} - \text{First RL}$ checks the *internal* calculations; the *closing error* is computed against the known closing BM. The student should report the closing error here as **+0.750 m**, indicating a probable levelling error in the field. The survey would normally be repeated.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

From the reduced levels table:

| **Station** | **RL (m)** |
|:-----------:|:----------:|
| 1 | 150.000 |
| 2 | 150.525 |
| 3 | 150.205 |
| 4 | 149.840 |
| 5 | 151.045 |
| 6 | 151.370 |
| 7 | 150.580 |
| 8 | 150.435 |
| 9 | 150.570 |

- **Highest Point:** Station 6, RL = 151.370 m.
- **Lowest Point:** Station 4, RL = 149.840 m.
- **Horizontal distance** (assuming uniform 20 m interval): 6 − 4 = 2 intervals = **40 m**.

$$
G = \dfrac{151.370 - 149.840}{40} = \dfrac{1.530}{40} = 0.03825 = 1 \text{ in } 26.14
$$

**Comment on Suitability:**
The ruling gradient for a metalled road is **1 in 30**. Our computed gradient of 1 in 26.14 is **steeper** than the ruling gradient (i.e., the denominator 26.14 < 30, so it rises faster). Therefore, the gradient is **not suitable** for a metalled road. Either the road has to be re-aligned (longer route) or the design has to be revised to provide a maximum gradient of 1 in 30.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches:
1. The procedure of **Profile Levelling** along a proposed road.
2. The procedure of **Cross Sectioning** at a given chainage.
3. List the **instruments** and **personnel** required for the field work.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cross-section at chainage 120 m of a road has the following staff readings with HI = 102.450 m. The formation width is 8 m with side slopes 1 : 1.

| **Offset (m)** | **L6** | **L3** | **CL 0** | **R3** | **R6** |
|:--------------:|:------:|:------:|:--------:|:------:|:------:|
| Staff reading (m) | 0.85 | 1.20 | 1.65 | 2.10 | 2.85 |

The proposed formation level is **100.500 m**. Compute the **depth of cut / fill** at each point and the **cross-sectional area** of earthwork using the **trapezoidal rule**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Procedure of Profile Levelling:**

1. Conduct reconnaissance and fix the centre line of the proposed road on the ground using ranging rods.
2. Mark chainages at regular intervals (e.g., 20 m) using a chain / tape.
3. Identify a permanent benchmark (BM) near the alignment. If none, establish one by fly levelling from a GTS benchmark.
4. Set up a dumpy level / automatic level on firm ground, midway between the staff stations, ensuring that the line of collimation is horizontal (bubble centred).
5. Take a **Backsight (BS)** on the BM and record in the level book. Compute the **Height of Instrument (HI)**.
6. Take **Intermediate Sights (IS)** on staff held at each successive chainage. Record readings.
7. At a Change Point (CP), take a **Foresight (FS)**, shift the instrument to a new position, and take a **Backsight (BS)** on the same CP to compute the new HI.
8. Continue until the line ends. Close the run on a known BM to check for closure error.
9. Reduce all levels using the HI method. Plot the L-Section on graph paper.

**2. Procedure of Cross Sectioning:**

1. At a chosen chainage (e.g., 120 m), the staff is held vertically on the centreline (CL = 0 m offset) and a reading is taken.
2. The staff is then moved to offsets of 1 m, 2 m, 3 m … up to the required width (e.g., 6 m) on the **left** of the centreline, and readings are taken.
3. The procedure is repeated on the **right** of the centreline.
4. All readings are taken from a single instrument setup if possible (to avoid change of HI errors).
5. The readings are booked in the level book under the "Cross Section" heading, and reduced using the HI method to obtain RL at each offset.
6. The cross-section is plotted on graph paper to scale.

**3. Instruments and Personnel:**

| **Instruments** | **Personnel** |
|:----------------|:--------------|
| Dumpy level / Automatic level (with tripod) | Instrument man (level operator) |
| Levelling staff (5 m or telescopic) | Staff man (chainman 1) |
| Ranging rods | Chainman 2 (for offsets) |
| Chain / tape (20 m / 30 m) | Book keeper (records readings) |
| Plumb bob, spirit level | |
| Level book, pencil, eraser | |
| Graph paper for plotting | |

**Personnel required:** Minimum **3 to 4 persons** (instrument man, 2 chainmen / staffmen, and 1 bookkeeper).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute RL of each cross-section point using $\text{RL} = \text{HI} - \text{Staff Reading}$.

$$
\text{RL}_{L6} = 102.450 - 0.85 = 101.600 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{RL}_{L3} = 102.450 - 1.20 = 101.250 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{RL}_{CL} = 102.450 - 1.65 = 100.800 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{RL}_{R3} = 102.450 - 2.10 = 100.350 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\text{RL}_{R6} = 102.450 - 2.85 = 99.600 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute depth of cut / fill = NGL − FL, where FL = 100.500 m.

| **Offset** | **NGL (m)** | **FL (m)** | **NGL − FL (m)** | **Cut (–) / Fill (+)** |
|:----------:|:-----------:|:----------:|:-----------------:|:----------------------:|
| L6 | 101.600 | 100.500 | +1.100 | Fill |
| L3 | 101.250 | 100.500 | +0.750 | Fill |
| CL | 100.800 | 100.500 | +0.300 | Fill |
| R3 | 100.350 | 100.500 | -0.150 | Cut |
| R6 | 99.600 | 100.500 | -0.900 | Cut |

**Step 3:** Cross-sectional area using trapezoidal rule (offset interval $d = 3$ m).

The ground profile crosses the formation level between CL and R3. To simplify, and per the question, we treat all areas as a single continuous net area:

$$
A_{L6 \to L3} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times (1.100 + 0.750) = 1.5 \times 1.850 = 2.775 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{L3 \to CL} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times (0.750 + 0.300) = 1.5 \times 1.050 = 1.575 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{CL \to R3} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times (0.300 + (-0.150)) = 1.5 \times 0.150 = 0.225 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{R3 \to R6} = \dfrac{3}{2} \times ((-0.150) + (-0.900)) = 1.5 \times (-1.050) = -1.575 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{\text{net}} = 2.775 + 1.575 + 0.225 - 1.575 = 3.000 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Alternative — Separate Cut and Fill Areas:**
> The convention in KTU valuation is to report **fill area** and **cut area** separately. Using the sign of the depth:
> - **Fill area** (positive depths from L6 → CL, and including the triangular portion CL to zero-crossing at R3):
>   - The zero-crossing (where NGL = FL) occurs between CL (depth = +0.300) and R3 (depth = -0.150). By linear interpolation at distance $x$ from CL: $0.300 - x \cdot \dfrac{0.300 + 0.150}{3} = 0$, hence $x = \dfrac{0.300 \times 3}{0.450} = 2.0 \text{ m}$. So zero-crossing is at offset = 2.0 m on the right.
>   - Fill area = $2.775 + 1.575 + \dfrac{2.0}{2} \times 0.300 = 2.775 + 1.575 + 0.300 = 4.650 \text{ m}^2$.
> - **Cut area** (R3 to R6 plus the small triangle from 2.0 m to R3):
>   - Cut area = $\dfrac{(3-2)}{2} \times 0.150 + \dfrac{3}{2} \times (0.150 + 0.900) = 0.075 + 1.575 = 1.650 \text{ m}^2$.

**Final Answer:** Fill Area = **4.650 m²**, Cut Area = **1.650 m²**, Net Area = **3.000 m²**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **HI sign error:** Forgetting to **add** BS and **subtract** FS/IS. Always remember: $\text{HI} = \text{RL} + \text{BS}$ and $\text{RL} = \text{HI} - \text{reading}$.
> 2. **Confusing IS and FS at Change Points:** At a CP, the reading of the *first* setup is FS, and the reading of the *second* setup is BS on the *same* point. The RL of the CP must be the same when computed by both setups.
> 3. **Forgetting the arithmetic check:** A simple $\Sigma BS - \Sigma FS = \text{Last RL} - \text{First RL}$ test will save you from arithmetic slips. Always perform it before submission.
> 4. **Not separating cut and fill:** KTU examiners award **dedicated marks** for explicitly identifying and tabulating cut and fill depths and computing cut area and fill area separately.
> 5. **Wrong plotting scale:** Using same scale for L-section and X-section; remember vertical exaggeration is **mandatory** for L-section.
> 6. **Neglecting closure error:** Always report the closure error and state whether the survey meets permissible tolerance ($12\sqrt{k}$ mm for ordinary levelling).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Profile Levelling** is differential levelling along a defined alignment to obtain an L-Section.
- **Cross Sectioning** is levelling perpendicular to the centreline to obtain an X-Section.
- **HI Method** is preferred in KTU practicals: $\text{HI} = \text{RL}_{\text{BM}} + \text{BS}$ and $\text{RL} = \text{HI} - \text{reading}$.
- **Arithmetic Check (Hinge Property):** $\Sigma BS - \Sigma FS = \text{Last RL} - \text{First RL}$.
- **BS** = first reading on a point of known RL within a setup. **FS** = last reading in a setup, taken on the CP. **IS** = any other reading in the same setup.
- **Gradient** $G = \dfrac{\Delta h}{\text{Horizontal distance}}$; expressed as **1 in n** rising or falling.
- **Formation Level (FL)** is the proposed finished surface level. **NGL** is the existing natural ground level.
- **Cut** occurs when NGL > FL; **Fill** when NGL < FL.
- **Trapezoidal Rule for area:** $A = \dfrac{d}{2}(y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + \dots)$ between adjacent offsets.
- **Volume by Trapezoidal Method:** $V = L \cdot \dfrac{A_1 + A_2}{2}$.
- **Volume by Prismoidal Method** (more accurate): $V = \dfrac{L}{6}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$.
- **Permissible error** in levelling: $\pm 12\sqrt{k}$ mm for ordinary work, where $k$ is in km.
- **Plotting scales:** L-Section — Horizontal 1 cm = 10 m, Vertical 1 cm = 1 m; X-Section — true scale 1 cm = 1 m.
- **Personnel:** Minimum 3 persons (instrument man, staff man, book keeper).
- **Instruments:** Dumpy / automatic level, levelling staff, chain, ranging rods, plumb bob, level book.
- **Always close the run on a known BM** to detect and report closure error.
- **Standard chainage interval** for L-Section: 20 m, 30 m, or 50 m. **Cross-section width**: extend until the formation slope line meets the natural ground.
- **Sign convention in cut / fill:** Positive difference (NGL − FL) → Fill, negative → Cut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL307 - SURVEY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Distance Between Inaccessible Points (Horizontal Angle) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distance Between Inaccessible Points (Horizontal Angle Method)** is a trigonometric surveying technique used to determine the horizontal separation between two points (say **P** and **Q**) whose physical occupation is **not feasible** — due to obstacles such as rivers, deep valleys, swamps, busy highways, restricted private property, or active construction sites — by measuring a **base line** between two accessible instrument stations and observing **horizontal angles** to both target points using a **theodolite** (or total station). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the experiment is formally catalogued as a "**horizontal control extension by triangulation**" using a **simple braced quadrilateral** or **two-triangle** configuration. The method is fundamentally an application of the **Law of Sines** and the **Law of Cosines** in the horizontal plane. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Two-Firefighter" Analogy** 🔥🚒 > > Imagine two firefighters standing on opposite banks of a wide river (**Base line AB**). They spot a house on fire across the river (**Point P**) and another burning hut further down (**Point Q**). They cannot swim across (inaccessible). However, each firefighter has a compass-theodolite. They aim at both fires and note the **angle** their telescope makes from a known reference. > > With the base line (river width) known, and four angles measured, they can — purely with paper and trigonometry — calculate how far apart the two fires are. **This is exactly what we do in this experiment.** The river is the obstacle, the base line is the measurable distance, and the angles are the horizontal angles read off the horizontal circle of the theodolite. ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities - **Base Line (AB)** — measured using a **steel tape** or **EDM**, typically **30 m to 100 m**. - **Horizontal Angles (α₁, α₂, β₁, β₂)** — measured using the **vernier theodolite** with a least count of typically **20″** or **1′**. - **Standard Assumed Values** — no atmospheric corrections needed (horizontal plane work, no vertical refraction considered at KTU level). ## 1.4 When is This Method Used? | Condition | Applicability | |---|---| | Both points inaccessible (cannot occupy) | ✅ Ideal | | Both points inter-visible from base line ends | ✅ Required | | Base line can be measured on ground | ✅ Required | | Angles are reasonably conditioned (not too acute) | ⚠️ Recommended | | Need high accuracy over short distances | ✅ Excellent | | Long distance (>1 km) with handheld tape | ❌ Use triangulation network | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Geometry — The Two-Triangle System The problem reduces to solving **two triangles sharing a common base** (AB) and then computing the third side of a third triangle formed by the two unknown points. ### 2.1.1 Operational Logic Steps 1. **Station Setup:** Erect theodolite at two accessible points **A** and **B**; measure the **baseline AB** directly on the ground. 2. **Centering & Levelling:** Ensure the instrument is perfectly centred over the station mark and the vertical axis is truly plumb (use optical plummet or plumb bob). 3. **Zero-Setting:** With the **horizontal circle reading 0°00′00″**, sight a reference object (usually the other station). 4. **Angle Observation at A:** Measure horizontal angles to **P** (call it **α₁**) and to **Q** (call it **α₂**). The included angle **∠PAQ = α₂ − α₁** (Case 1) or **α₁ + α₂** (Case 2). 5. **Angle Observation at B:** Similarly measure horizontal angles to **P** (call it **β₁**) and to **Q** (call it **β₂**). 6. **Face Change:** Repeat the readings with **telescope inverted (Face Left → Face Right)** to eliminate instrumental errors (collimation, trunnion axis error). 7. **Trigonometric Computation:** Apply the **sine rule** in △APB and △AQB to obtain AP, BP, AQ, BQ. Then use the **cosine rule** in △APQ to obtain **PQ**. ## 2.2 The Two Standard Cases > [!NOTE] > **CASE 1 — Both points P and Q lie on the SAME SIDE of baseline AB** (most common in KTU lab). > **CASE 2 — Points P and Q lie on OPPOSITE SIDES of baseline AB.** ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — every KTU question derives directly from it.** | # | Triangle | Known | Required | Formula | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | △APB | AB, α₁, β₁ | AP | $AP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin(\beta_1)}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$ | | 2 | △APB | AB, α₁, β₁ | BP | $BP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin(\alpha_1)}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$ | | 3 | △AQB | AB, α₂, β₂ | AQ | $AQ = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin(\beta_2)}{\sin(\alpha_2 + \beta_2)}$ | | 4 | △AQB | AB, α₂, β₂ | BQ | $BQ = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin(\alpha_2)}{\sin(\alpha_2 + \beta_2)}$ | | 5 | △APQ (Case 1) | AP, AQ, ∠PAQ = α₂ − α₁ | PQ | $PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1)$ | | 6 | △APQ (Case 2) | AP, AQ, ∠PAQ = α₁ + α₂ | PQ | $PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)$ | | 7 | Sine Rule (general) | a, A, B | b | $\dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} = \dfrac{c}{\sin C}$ | | 8 | Cosine Rule (general) | a, b, C | c | $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cdot \cos C$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bridge Construction:** Measuring the width of a deep river gorge to plan a cable-stayed bridge span. - **Transmission Line Tower Spacing:** Determining horizontal separation between two tower peaks on adjacent hillocks. - **Dam & Reservoir Engineering:** Computing reservoir width between two inaccessible cliff points. - **Military & Defence:** Range finding across minefields or hostile territory. - **Boundary Demarcation:** Resolving disputes where boundary stones are in marshes or on islands. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Sine-Rule Expressions for AP and BP **Consider △APB** with vertices A, P, B. Let the interior angles be: - $\angle PAB = \alpha_1$ (measured at station A to point P) - $\angle PBA = \beta_1$ (measured at station B to point P) - $\angle APB = 180° - (\alpha_1 + \beta_1)$ (angle sum property of a triangle) By the **Law of Sines** applied to △APB: $$ \dfrac{AP}{\sin(\angle PBA)} = \dfrac{BP}{\sin(\angle PAB)} = \dfrac{AB}{\sin(\angle APB)} $$ Substituting the known angles: $$ \dfrac{AP}{\sin \beta_1} = \dfrac{BP}{\sin \alpha_1} = \dfrac{AB}{\sin(180° - \alpha_1 - \beta_1)} $$ Since $\sin(180° - \theta) = \sin \theta$, the denominator simplifies: $$ \dfrac{AP}{\sin \beta_1} = \dfrac{BP}{\sin \alpha_1} = \dfrac{AB}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} $$ Solving for **AP** (cross-multiplication): $$ AP \cdot \sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1) = AB \cdot \sin \beta_1 $$ $$ AP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} \quad \text{(Result 1)} $$ Solving for **BP**: $$ BP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \alpha_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} \quad \text{(Result 2)} $$ > The same logic is applied to △AQB with angles α₂ and β₂ to get **AQ** and **BQ**. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Cosine-Rule Expression for PQ **Consider △APQ.** Two sides AP and AQ and the included angle $\angle PAQ$ are known. The included angle $\angle PAQ$ is obtained from the difference (or sum) of the two horizontal angles measured at A. For **Case 1** (same side): $$ \angle PAQ = \alpha_2 - \alpha_1 $$ For **Case 2** (opposite sides): $$ \angle PAQ = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 $$ Applying the **Law of Cosines** in △APQ: $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\angle PAQ) $$ **Substituting for Case 1:** $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \quad \text{(Final Answer)} $$ **Substituting for Case 2:** $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) \quad \text{(Final Answer)} $$ ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Fully Solved) **Given Data:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Base line AB | 60.000 m | | Angle α₁ = ∠PAB (at A to P) | 38° 24′ 20″ | | Angle α₂ = ∠QAB (at A to Q) | 72° 18′ 40″ | | Angle β₁ = ∠PBA (at B to P) | 51° 12′ 00″ | | Angle β₂ = ∠QBA (at B to Q) | 28° 45′ 30″ | | Configuration | Case 1 (same side) | **Step 1 — Convert to decimal degrees** for ease: $$ \alpha_1 = 38 + \tfrac{24}{60} + \tfrac{20}{3600} = 38.4056° $$ $$ \alpha_2 = 72 + \tfrac{18}{60} + \tfrac{40}{3600} = 72.3111° $$ $$ \beta_1 = 51 + \tfrac{12}{60} + 0 = 51.2000° $$ $$ \beta_2 = 28 + \tfrac{45}{60} + \tfrac{30}{3600} = 28.7583° $$ **Step 2 — Compute AP using the sine rule in △APB:** $$ AP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \sin(51.2000°)}{\sin(38.4056° + 51.2000°)} $$ Evaluating the sines: $$ \sin(51.2000°) = 0.77942, \quad \sin(89.6056°) = 0.99998 $$ $$ AP = \dfrac{60 \times 0.77942}{0.99998} = \dfrac{46.7652}{0.99998} = 46.766 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Compute BP:** $$ BP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \alpha_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)} = \dfrac{60 \times \sin(38.4056°)}{0.99998} $$ $$ \sin(38.4056°) = 0.62123 $$ $$ BP = \dfrac{60 \times 0.62123}{0.99998} = \dfrac{37.2738}{0.99998} = 37.274 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Compute AQ using the sine rule in △AQB:** $$ AQ = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \beta_2}{\sin(\alpha_2 + \beta_2)} = \dfrac{60 \cdot \sin(28.7583°)}{\sin(72.3111° + 28.7583°)} $$ Evaluating: $$ \sin(28.7583°) = 0.48100, \quad \sin(101.0694°) = \sin(78.9306°) = 0.98132 $$ $$ AQ = \dfrac{60 \times 0.48100}{0.98132} = \dfrac{28.860}{0.98132} = 29.411 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the included angle ∠PAQ (Case 1):** $$ \angle PAQ = \alpha_2 - \alpha_1 = 72.3111° - 38.4056° = 33.9055° $$ **Step 6 — Compute PQ using the cosine rule in △APQ:** $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\angle PAQ) $$ $$ PQ^2 = (46.766)^2 + (29.411)^2 - 2 \times 46.766 \times 29.411 \times \cos(33.9055°) $$ Computing each term: $$ (46.766)^2 = 2187.06 $$ $$ (29.411)^2 = 865.00 $$ $$ 2 \times 46.766 \times 29.411 = 2751.22 $$ $$ \cos(33.9055°) = 0.82958 $$ $$ PQ^2 = 2187.06 + 865.00 - 2751.22 \times 0.82958 $$ $$ PQ^2 = 3052.06 - 2282.30 = 769.76 $$ $$ PQ = \sqrt{769.76} = 27.745 \text{ m} $$ ### ✅ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{PQ = 27.745 \text{ m}}$ ## 3.4 Error Analysis & Accuracy > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024, mention these in your record: > - The **base line** is the largest source of error — measure it twice (forward and backward) and average. > - The **weakest geometric condition** is when α₁ + β₁ is close to 180° (very long narrow triangle) — avoid this. > - KTU demands **face-left and face-right** readings to cancel collimation errors. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Geometric Configuration — Case 1 (Same Side) ```mermaid graph TD stationA["Station A
Theodolite 1
alpha1 and alpha2
measured here"] stationB["Station B
Theodolite 2
beta1 and beta2
measured here"] pointP["Point P
Inaccessible
Target 1"] pointQ["Point Q
Inaccessible
Target 2"] baseAB["Baseline AB
Measured on ground
Length L"] stationA ---|"alpha1"| pointP stationA ---|"alpha2"| pointQ stationB ---|"beta1"| pointP stationB ---|"beta2"| pointQ stationA ---|"Length L"| baseAB baseAB ---|"Length L"| stationB pointP ---|"Distance PQ
To find"| pointQ ``` ## 4.2 Geometric Configuration — Case 2 (Opposite Sides) ```mermaid graph TD stationA2["Station A"] stationB2["Station B"] pointP2["Point P
One side of AB"] pointQ2["Point Q
Opposite side of AB"] baseAB2["Baseline AB"] stationA2 ---|"alpha1"| pointP2 stationA2 ---|"alpha2"| pointQ2 stationB2 ---|"beta1"| pointP2 stationB2 ---|"beta2"| pointQ2 stationA2 ---|"L"| baseAB2 baseAB2 ---|"L"| stationB2 pointP2 ---|"PQ to find"| pointQ2 ``` ## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD start(["Start Experiment"]) --> step1["Step 1: Select two accessible
stations A and B with
clear intervisibility"] step1 --> step2["Step 2: Measure baseline AB
using steel tape
Forward and Backward"] step2 --> step3["Step 3: Set up theodolite at A
Centre, level, orient"] step3 --> step4["Step 4: Sight B with HR zero
Then sight P to read alpha1
Then sight Q to read alpha2"] step4 --> step5["Step 5: Transverse instrument
to station B"] step5 --> step6["Step 6: Set up theodolite at B
Centre, level, orient to A"] step6 --> step7["Step 7: Sight A with HR zero
Then sight P to read beta1
Then sight Q to read beta2"] step7 --> step8["Step 8: Repeat all readings
on Face Right
Average the two faces"] step8 --> step9["Step 9: Apply Sine Rule
in triangle APB and AQB
to get AP, BP, AQ, BQ"] step9 --> step10["Step 10: Apply Cosine Rule
in triangle APQ to get PQ"] step10 --> end(["Record Result and
Plot Diagram"]) style start fill:#90EE90 style end fill:#FFB6C1 style step2 fill:#FFE4B5 style step8 fill:#FFE4B5 style step10 fill:#ADD8E6 ``` ## 4.4 Data Recording Table (Template) ```mermaid graph LR tbl["Observation Table"] --> col1["Column 1
Instrument Station"] tbl --> col2["Column 2
Target Sighted"] tbl --> col3["Column 3
Face Left Reading"] tbl --> col4["Column 4
Face Right Reading"] tbl --> col5["Column 5
Mean Horizontal Angle"] tbl --> col6["Column 6
Remarks"] style tbl fill:#E6E6FA ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define inaccessible points in surveying. List the instruments used to determine the distance between them by the horizontal angle method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Inaccessible points are those station points on the ground that **cannot be physically occupied** by the surveyor or instrument due to obstacles such as rivers, marshes, cliffs, or restricted zones. > > The **horizontal angle method** employs: > 1. A **vernier theodolite** (or total station) — for measuring horizontal angles. > 2. A **steel tape** or **electronic distance measurement (EDM)** device — for measuring the base line. > > The principle is to apply the **sine rule** and **cosine rule** in a system of triangles sharing the measured base line. > *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for instruments, 1 for principle)* ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines as used in the inaccessible points problem.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Law of Sines:** In any triangle with sides a, b, c opposite to angles A, B, C: > $$\dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} = \dfrac{c}{\sin C}$$ > > **Law of Cosines:** For the same triangle, the side c opposite to angle C is given by: > $$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$$ > > In this experiment, the Law of Sines is used in △APB and △AQB to compute the unknown sides AP, BP, AQ, BQ, while the Law of Cosines is used in △APQ to compute the final answer PQ. > *(3 marks: 1 each for stating both laws and 1 for identifying their application)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### 🔵 Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A baseline AB = 80 m is measured between two theodolite stations. Horizontal angles observed are: at A, ∠PAB = 42° 18′ and ∠QAB = 76° 42′; at B, ∠PBA = 58° 24′ and ∠QBA = 32° 10′. Points P and Q are on the same side of AB. Determine the distance PQ. **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the two cases (same side and opposite side) of the inaccessible points problem and state the formula used in each. #### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Step 1:** Convert angles to decimal degrees. $$ \alpha_1 = 42 + \tfrac{18}{60} = 42.3000° $$ $$ \alpha_2 = 76 + \tfrac{42}{60} = 76.7000° $$ $$ \beta_1 = 58 + \tfrac{24}{60} = 58.4000° $$ $$ \beta_2 = 32 + \tfrac{10}{60} = 32.1667° $$ **Step 2:** Compute AP (sine rule in △APB). $$ AP = \dfrac{80 \cdot \sin(58.4000°)}{\sin(42.3000° + 58.4000°)} = \dfrac{80 \times 0.85167}{\sin(100.7000°)} $$ $$ \sin(100.7000°) = \sin(79.3000°) = 0.98249 $$ $$ AP = \dfrac{68.134}{0.98249} = 69.346 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3:** Compute AQ (sine rule in △AQB). $$ AQ = \dfrac{80 \cdot \sin(32.1667°)}{\sin(76.7000° + 32.1667°)} = \dfrac{80 \times 0.53219}{\sin(108.8667°)} $$ $$ \sin(108.8667°) = \sin(71.1333°) = 0.94623 $$ $$ AQ = \dfrac{42.575}{0.94623} = 44.994 \text{ m} \approx 45.00 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4:** Compute the included angle (Case 1). $$ \angle PAQ = \alpha_2 - \alpha_1 = 76.7000° - 42.3000° = 34.4000° $$ **Step 5:** Apply cosine rule in △APQ. $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(34.4000°) $$ $$ PQ^2 = (69.346)^2 + (45.000)^2 - 2 \times 69.346 \times 45.000 \times \cos(34.4000°) $$ $$ PQ^2 = 4808.87 + 2025.00 - 6241.14 \times 0.82511 $$ $$ PQ^2 = 6833.87 - 5149.27 = 1684.60 $$ $$ \boxed{PQ = \sqrt{1684.60} = 41.045 \text{ m}} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for sine rule application, 2 for cosine rule application, 2 for correct computation, 1 for final answer with units)* #### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Sketch (Same Side — Case 1):** ``` P /|\ / | \ / | \ / | \ A----B----Q (baseline) ``` All three points P, Q lie above the baseline AB, on the **same side**. **Sketch (Opposite Side — Case 2):** ``` P Q \ / \ / \ / A--------B (baseline) ``` P and Q lie on **opposite sides** of baseline AB. **Formulas:** For **Case 1 (same side)**: $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) $$ For **Case 2 (opposite side)**: $$ PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2) $$ The AP and AQ values are computed identically via the sine rule in both cases; only the included angle formula differs. *(7 marks: 2 for each sketch, 1 for identifying cases, 2 for correct formulas)* --- ### 🟣 Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Two inaccessible points P and Q are situated on opposite sides of a baseline AB = 50 m. The following horizontal angles are measured: at A, ∠PAB = 36° 12′ and ∠QAB = 65° 48′; at B, ∠PBA = 72° 30′ and ∠QBA = 41° 18′. Calculate the distance PQ. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step procedure of the inaccessible points experiment in the field, including centering, levelling, face change, and the need for a well-conditioned triangle. #### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Step 1:** Convert angles. $$ \alpha_1 = 36.2000°, \quad \alpha_2 = 65.8000° $$ $$ \beta_1 = 72.5000°, \quad \beta_2 = 41.3000° $$ **Step 2:** Compute AP. $$ AP = \dfrac{50 \cdot \sin(72.5000°)}{\sin(36.2000° + 72.5000°)} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.95372}{\sin(108.7000°)} $$ $$ \sin(108.7000°) = \sin(71.3000°) = 0.94693 $$ $$ AP = \dfrac{47.686}{0.94693} = 50.355 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3:** Compute AQ. $$ AQ = \dfrac{50 \cdot \sin(41.3000°)}{\sin(65.8000° + 41.3000°)} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.65935}{\sin(107.1000°)} $$ $$ \sin(107.1000°) = \sin(72.9000°) = 0.95630 $$ $$ AQ = \dfrac{32.968}{0.95630} = 34.473 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4:** Included angle (Case 2 — opposite sides). $$ \angle PAQ = \alpha_1 + \alpha_2 = 36.2000° + 65.8000° = 102.0000° $$ **Step 5:** Apply cosine rule. $$ PQ^2 = (50.355)^2 + (34.473)^2 - 2 \times 50.355 \times 34.473 \times \cos(102.0000°) $$ $$ \cos(102.0000°) = -0.20791 $$ $$ PQ^2 = 2535.63 + 1188.45 - 3471.78 \times (-0.20791) $$ $$ PQ^2 = 3724.08 + 721.79 = 4445.87 $$ $$ \boxed{PQ = \sqrt{4445.87} = 66.678 \text{ m}} $$ *(7 marks: distributed across angle conversion, sine rule, cosine rule, and final computation)* #### 🟢 Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Field Procedure:** 1. **Reconnaissance:** Walk the site, identify two accessible, mutually inter-visible stations A and B on firm ground where the inaccessible points P and Q are also visible. 2. **Baseline Marking:** Drive ranging rods or wooden pegs at A and B. Ensure the baseline length is well-conditioned (ideally AB ≥ 0.5 × PQ, and not exceeding 1.5 × PQ). 3. **Baseline Measurement:** Measure AB with a **steel tape** (or EDM). Apply: - Pull correction - Temperature correction (if temperature differs from standard 20°C) - Sag correction (if tape not fully supported) - Slope correction (if terrain is not level) - Take **at least two measurements** and average. 4. **Instrument Setup at A:** - **Centring:** Use plumb bob / optical plummet to place instrument exactly over station mark A. - **Levelling:** Use the **alidade bubble** and the **plate bubble** to make the vertical axis truly plumb. - **Orientation:** Set horizontal circle to 0°00′00″ and sight station B. 5. **Angle Measurement at A (Face Left):** - Loosen upper clamp, sight P → read α₁. - Loosen upper clamp, sight Q → read α₂. - Compute included angle = α₂ − α₁ (Case 1) or α₁ + α₂ (Case 2). 6. **Face Change and Repeat:** Invert the telescope (Face Right). Repeat step 5. **Average** the Face Left and Face Right readings to eliminate: - **Collimation error** - **Trunnion axis error** - **Index error** of vertical circle (not relevant here, but conceptually) 7. **Shift Instrument to B:** Carefully dismantle and re-setup at B. Repeat steps 4–6 to get β₁ and β₂. 8. **Well-Conditioned Triangle Criterion:** The angle at the apex (P or Q) should ideally lie between **30° and 120°** to avoid the **weak geometry problem**, which amplifies small angular errors into large linear errors. 9. **Computation:** Reduce data and apply the sine rule and cosine rule as derived. 10. **Plotting & Verification:** Plot the result on a graph sheet using a suitable scale to visually verify. *(7 marks: 1 for reconnaissance, 2 for instrument setup, 2 for angle measurement with face change, 1 for well-conditioned triangle, 1 for computation step)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that KTU examiners repeatedly penalize — DO NOT commit these:** > > 1. **Angle conversion error:** Forgetting to convert **minutes to decimal degrees** (or vice versa). Marks are deducted for inconsistent units. > 2. **Wrong included angle:** Mixing up **Case 1 and Case 2** — students blindly use $α₂ − α₁$ even when points are on opposite sides. **Always check the field sketch first.** > 3. **Forgetting $\sin(180°-θ) = \sin θ$:** Many students write $\sin(180°-α₁-β₁)$ and then get stuck. Simplify it. > 4. **Calculator mode error:** Ensure the calculator is in **DEG mode**, not RAD or GRAD. > 5. **No face change:** Mentioning only one face reading costs you 1 mark easily. > 6. **Units missing:** Final answer must carry **units (m or metres)** — examiners are strict. > 7. **Weak triangle geometry:** If the apex angle falls below 20°, the cosine rule amplifies errors — examiners will mark down if the result is suspiciously inaccurate. > 8. **Mis-spelling "vernier theodolite"** as "vernier theodolite" vs "vernier theodolite" — silly but it happens. > 9. **Not labelling the sketch properly:** The two cases MUST have a diagram with P, Q, A, B clearly marked and angles α₁, α₂, β₁, β₂ written on the appropriate rays. > 10. **Mixing P and Q:** Carefully re-read the question — sometimes the question asks for **BP and BQ**, not PQ. Don't blindly apply the cosine rule at A. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize before entering the exam hall.** > > ✅ **Core Idea:** A baseline is measured on accessible ground; horizontal angles are shot from both ends to two inaccessible points; trigonometry gives the unknown distance. > > ✅ **Two Triangles:** △APB and △AQB share side AB. > > ✅ **Third Triangle:** △APQ is the one whose side PQ we need. > > ✅ **Two Cases:** > - **Same side** → included angle = α₂ − α₁ > - **Opposite side** → included angle = α₁ + α₂ > > ✅ **Sine Rule Formula (sides of the two known triangles):** > $$AP = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \beta_1}{\sin(\alpha_1 + \beta_1)}$$ > $$AQ = \dfrac{AB \cdot \sin \beta_2}{\sin(\alpha_2 + \beta_2)}$$ > > ✅ **Cosine Rule Formula (final answer):** > $$PQ^2 = AP^2 + AQ^2 - 2 \cdot AP \cdot AQ \cdot \cos(\angle PAQ)$$ > > ✅ **Instruments:** Vernier theodolite + steel tape / EDM. > > ✅ **Always use both faces (L and R)** to eliminate instrumental errors. > > ✅ **Baseline should be conditioned:** Length of AB ≈ distance to the target points; apex angle between 30° and 120°. > > ✅ **Convert all angles to decimal degrees** (or all to deg-min-sec) and keep consistent. > > ✅ **Final answer must include units** — typically **metres (m)**. > > ✅ **Accuracy tips:** Measure baseline twice (forward + backward); use a 20″ or 1′ least-count theodolite; check for plumb-bob centring. > > ✅ **Applications:** Bridge spans, river widths, tower separations, boundary disputes across obstacles.

topics.title: Level Difference Between Points (Vertical Angle)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL307 - SURVEY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Level Difference Between Points (Vertical Angle)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trigonometric Levelling (Vertical Angle Method):**
> Trigonometric levelling is the process of determining the **differences in elevation (level difference)** of two points by measuring the **vertical angle** subtended by the line of sight at the instrument station and the **horizontal distance** between the two stations, using the principles of trigonometry. The method is most suitable in **hilly, undulating, or inaccessible terrain** where direct spirit levelling becomes tedious, time-consuming, or practically impossible.

**Key Terminology:**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Instrument Station (I.S.)** | The point on the ground where the theodolite is set up and perfectly levelled. |
| **Staff Station (S.S.)** | The point whose reduced level (R.L.) is to be determined; a levelling staff is held vertically over it. |
| **Vertical Angle (θ)** | The angle measured by the theodolite in the vertical plane between the horizontal line of sight and the inclined line of sight to the staff. |
| **Reduced Level (R.L.)** | The vertical distance of a point above or below an arbitrary datum (mean sea level is the standard datum for KTU practicals). |
| **Height of Instrument (hᵢ)** | The vertical distance from the ground point (instrument station) to the centre of the theodolite's horizontal axis (trunnion axis). |
| **Staff Reading (r)** | The reading observed on the levelling staff through the central cross-hair when sighted from the instrument. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Firefighter's Ladder"**
> Imagine a firefighter standing on the ground and looking up at a person stranded on a balcony. The firefighter knows how far horizontally she is standing from the building (say, 8 m). She also uses an angle-measuring device on her helmet to find that she must tilt her head up at an angle of 30° to look at the person. Using basic trigonometry, she can instantly calculate how high the balcony is — without ever climbing a ladder or dropping a measuring tape.
> 
> In surveying, the theodolite plays the role of the firefighter's helmet, the horizontal distance is the firefighter's distance from the building, the vertical angle is the head-tilt, and the staff is the person on the balcony. The **level difference** is simply the height of that "balcony" calculated via trig.

### 1.3 Why We Use This Method

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Justification:**
> - **Speed:** A single theodolite setup covers hundreds of metres in minutes.
> - **Accessibility:** Works where levelling staff cannot be held vertically (e.g., across rivers, steep cliffs, transmission towers).
> - **Long Range:** Combined with electronic distance measurement (EDM), trigonometric levelling can determine elevations across **kilometres**.
> - **Cost-Effective for Rough Surveys:** Best suited for preliminary route alignment, contouring in hilly regions, and reconnaissance surveys.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Practicals

- **Distance unit:** Metre (**m**) — KTU lab standard.
- **Angle unit:** Degree (°), Minute (′), Second (″).
- **Datum:** Arbitrary (100.000 m) is generally assumed unless a known B.M. is given.
- **Minimum two faces** (Face Left + Face Right) of the theodolite must be used to eliminate instrumental errors.
---

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The method rests on a simple right-angled triangle in the **vertical plane** of observation:

1. **Setup the theodolite** over the instrument station, level it precisely, and measure the **height of instrument (hᵢ)** from the ground mark to the trunnion (horizontal) axis.
2. **Hold a levelling staff** vertically on the staff station.
3. **Sight the staff** through the telescope, with the vertical circle reading the **vertical angle (θ)** and the central cross-hair giving the **staff reading (r)**.
4. **Measure the horizontal distance (s)** between the two stations. This is the base of the right-angled triangle.
5. **Apply trigonometry** to compute the vertical component (V) of the inclined sight, then add/subtract V, r, and hᵢ to obtain the level difference.

### 2.2 The Core Equation — Built Step by Step

Consider the instrument at station **A** and staff at **B**, with the line of sight inclined **upwards** (vertical angle = +θ, i.e., elevation).

**Step 1 — Vertical component of inclined sight:**
$$V = s \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 2 — Elevation of line of sight above the instrument axis at the staff station:**
$$\text{Elevation} = V + r$$

**Step 3 — Elevation of line of sight above the staff station ground:**
$$\text{Elevation} = V + r$$

**Step 4 — R.L. of staff station B:**
$$\text{R.L. of B} = \text{R.L. of A} + h_i + V - r$$

**Final Master Equation (for elevation, +θ):**
$$\boxed{\text{R.L. of B} = \text{R.L. of A} + h_i + s \cdot \tan\theta - r}$$

For **depression** (vertical angle is negative, –θ), the sign changes:
$$\boxed{\text{R.L. of B} = \text{R.L. of A} + h_i - s \cdot \tan\theta - r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Convention):**
> - **Elevation angle = Positive (+)** — Staff station is higher than the instrument axis.
> - **Depression angle = Negative (–)** — Staff station is lower than the instrument axis.
> - **"Angle of Elevation"** and **"Angle of Depression"** are NOT the same; always note which vertical plane and which face.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Reference Table — Print and Pin to Your Record Book**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical distance (V) | $V = s \cdot \tan\theta$ | m | Base × tan of vertical angle |
| 2 | V from slope distance (l) | $V = l \cdot \sin\theta$ | m | When slope distance is measured |
| 3 | Horizontal distance (s) | $s = l \cdot \cos\theta$ | m | From slope distance |
| 4 | R.L. of staff station (elevation case) | $\text{R.L.}_B = \text{R.L.}_A + h_i + s\tan\theta - r$ | m | When staff is higher |
| 5 | R.L. of staff station (depression case) | $\text{R.L.}_B = \text{R.L.}_A + h_i - s\tan\theta - r$ | m | When staff is lower |
| 6 | Level difference between A and B | $\Delta h = \text{R.L.}_B - \text{R.L.}_A$ | m | +ve if B is higher |
| 7 | Reciprocal observation (mean) | $\Delta h = \dfrac{\Delta h_{AB} - \Delta h_{BA}}{2}$ | m | Eliminates curvature, refraction, and instrumental errors |
| 8 | Combined correction (R.L. due to curvature & refraction) | $C = 0.0675 \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{R}$ | m | $D$ in km, $R$ = Earth's radius ≈ 6370 km |
| 9 | Staff reading correction for non-vertical staff | $r_{\text{true}} = r \cdot \cos\alpha$ | m | $\alpha$ = tilt of staff from vertical |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Long-span bridge construction:** Determining pier elevations across rivers where direct levelling is impossible.
- **Transmission line tower surveys:** Finding differences in height across hilly terrain.
- **Dam and reservoir surveys:** Monitoring crest levels over large water bodies.
- **Tunnel and mine surveys:** Connecting surface and underground levels.
- **Aerial photogrammetry ground control:** Establishing vertical control points for mapping.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Formula

**Given:** Instrument at A, staff at B, vertical angle θ (elevation), horizontal distance s, height of instrument hᵢ, staff reading r.

**Diagram Description (Refer to SECTION 4 for visual):**
- A = Instrument station on ground.
- A′ = Top of theodolite (trunnion axis), located hᵢ above A.
- B = Staff station on ground.
- B′ = Point on the staff where the central cross-hair intersects it; the reading is r, measured **downward** from the top of the staff to B′.
- B″ = Projection of the trunnion axis on the staff along the horizontal direction.
- Line A′B′ = Inclined line of sight, making angle θ with horizontal.
- Horizontal line A′B″ (parallel to AB).

**Step 1:** In the right-angled triangle A′B″B′ (right-angled at B″),
- A′B″ = Horizontal distance = s
- A′B′ = Inclined line of sight = l (slope distance, if measured)
- Angle at A′ = θ

**Step 2:** Vertical component of line of sight:
$$V = A''B' = A'B'' \cdot \tan\theta = s \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 3:** The point B′ (where the line of sight cuts the staff) is at height (s·tanθ) above the horizontal line through A′.
$$A'B' \sin\theta = A'B'' \tan\theta = s \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 4:** The staff reading r is measured **from the foot of the staff (point B) to the point of intersection B′**, so B′ is at height r above the ground point B.

**Step 5:** Elevation of the trunnion axis (A′) above the staff station ground (B):
$$\text{Elevation}(A') = V + r = s \cdot \tan\theta + r$$

**Step 6:** R.L. of trunnion axis A′ above the datum:
$$\text{R.L.}(A') = \text{R.L.}(A) + h_i$$

**Step 7:** R.L. of staff station B:
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = \text{R.L.}(A') - (s \cdot \tan\theta + r)$$

**Step 8:** Substituting R.L.(A′) from Step 6:
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = \text{R.L.}(A) + h_i - s \cdot \tan\theta - r$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Error Alert (Most Common Mistake in KTU Records):**
> In Step 7 above, the term $(s \cdot \tan\theta + r)$ is **subtracted** from R.L.(A′) because B is on the **ground**, while A′ is the trunnion axis. The vertical distance from A′ DOWN to B = (s·tanθ + r). A common student error is to ADD this term, leading to wrong R.L. values.
> 
> **Alternative form used in KTU practicals** (most textbooks write it as a positive formula by rearranging):
> $$\text{R.L.}(B) = \text{R.L.}(A) + h_i + s \cdot \tan\theta - r$$
> Both are identical — verify the sign convention in your lab manual before submitting your record.

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Single Observation (KTU Typical)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A theodolite is set up at station A, whose R.L. is **125.500 m**. The height of instrument is **1.425 m**. The staff is held at station B, and the following observations are taken:
> - Horizontal distance from A to B = **50.000 m**
> - Vertical angle to staff = **+5°24′36″** (elevation)
> - Central hair reading on staff = **1.280 m**
> 
> Determine the R.L. of station B.

**Solution (Exhaustive — Every Step Written):**

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**
- R.L.(A) = 125.500 m
- hᵢ = 1.425 m
- s = 50.000 m
- θ = +5°24′36″
- r = 1.280 m

**Step 2 — Convert vertical angle to decimal degrees (for calculator):**
$$\theta = 5 + \dfrac{24}{60} + \dfrac{36}{3600} = 5 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 = 5.4100°$$

**Step 3 — Compute tan(θ):**
$$\tan(5.4100°) = 0.09469 \text{ (approximately)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute vertical component V:**
$$V = s \cdot \tan\theta = 50.000 \times 0.09469 = 4.7345 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the master formula (elevation case):**
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = \text{R.L.}(A) + h_i + V - r$$

**Step 6 — Substitute values:**
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = 125.500 + 1.425 + 4.7345 - 1.280$$

**Step 7 — Perform the arithmetic in proper order:**
$$125.500 + 1.425 = 126.925$$
$$126.925 + 4.7345 = 131.6595$$
$$131.6595 - 1.280 = 130.3795 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Final answer:**
$$\boxed{\text{R.L. of B} = 130.380 \text{ m (rounded to 3 decimals)}}$$

**Step 9 — Level difference:**
$$\Delta h = \text{R.L.}(B) - \text{R.L.}(A) = 130.380 - 125.500 = +4.880 \text{ m}$$

> **Interpretation:** Station B is **4.880 m higher** than station A.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Reciprocal Method (Advanced, KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem Statement:**
> Two stations A and B are on opposite banks of a river. Theodolite is set up at A (R.L. = 250.000 m) and observations are taken to staff at B, and vice versa. Data:
> 
> | Observation | From A to B | From B to A |
> |---|---|---|
> | Height of instrument (hᵢ) | 1.50 m | 1.55 m |
> | Vertical angle (θ) | +2°18′ | –2°20′ |
> | Staff reading (r) | 1.85 m | 1.80 m |
> | Horizontal distance (s) | 200 m | 200 m |
> 
> Find the true difference in level between A and B.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Δh from A to B (staff at B, instrument at A):**
$$\theta_1 = 2°18' = 2.3000°$$
$$\tan(2.3000°) = 0.04016$$
$$V_1 = 200 \times 0.04016 = 8.032 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = 250.000 + 1.50 + 8.032 - 1.85 = 257.682 \text{ m}$$
$$\Delta h_{AB} = 257.682 - 250.000 = +7.682 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Δh from B to A (staff at A, instrument at B):**
$$\theta_2 = 2°20' = 2.3333°$$
$$\tan(2.3333°) = 0.04075$$
$$V_2 = 200 \times 0.04075 = 8.150 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{R.L.}(A) = 257.682 + 1.55 - 8.150 - 1.80 = 249.282 \text{ m}$$
$$\Delta h_{BA} = 249.282 - 257.682 = -8.400 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the reciprocal mean formula:**
$$\Delta h_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{\Delta h_{AB} - \Delta h_{BA}}{2} = \dfrac{(+7.682) - (-8.400)}{2} = \dfrac{16.082}{2} = +8.041 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Final R.L. of B (using true Δh):**
$$\text{R.L.}(B) = 250.000 + 8.041 = 258.041 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Two Values Differ:** The +7.682 m and –8.400 m are different in magnitude because of **curvature of the earth**, **atmospheric refraction**, and minor **instrumental errors**. The reciprocal method averages these out, yielding the true difference.

### 3.4 Common Pitfalls — Analytical Checklist

> [!WARNING]
> - **Forgetting the ± sign** of the vertical angle is the #1 cause of full-mark loss.
> - **Mixing up s and l** (horizontal vs slope distance) — always check what the tape/EDM measured.
> - **Using degrees-minutes-seconds (DMS) directly** in a calculator expecting decimal degrees — convert first.
> - **Neglecting hᵢ and r** — even small errors (1 cm) can fail a 14-mark question.
> - **Not averaging Face Left and Face Right** readings — KTU strict rule: both faces must be observed.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify Instrument Station A and Staff Station B"] --> B["Set up theodolite at A and level it precisely"]
    B --> C["Measure Height of Instrument h_i using tape"]
    C --> D["Hold levelling staff vertically at B on a stable peg"]
    D --> E["Sight staff with telescope, observe vertical angle theta on vertical circle"]
    E --> F["Record staff reading r on central cross-hair"]
    F --> G["Measure horizontal distance s using tape or stadia"]
    G --> H["Apply face left and face right observations for accuracy"]
    H --> I{"Vertical angle is elevation or depression?"}
    I -->|Elevation plus theta| J["Compute V = s times tan theta"]
    I -->|Depression minus theta| K["Compute V = s times tan theta with negative sign"]
    J --> L["R.L. of B = R.L. of A plus h_i plus V minus r"]
    K --> M["R.L. of B = R.L. of A plus h_i minus V minus r"]
    L --> N["Level difference delta h = R.L. of B minus R.L. of A"]
    M --> N
    N --> O["Repeat with reciprocal setup to eliminate errors"]
    O --> P["Compute true mean level difference"]
    P --> Q["End: Report R.L. of staff station B with three decimal places"]
```

    B1 -->|"Vertical V = s tan theta"| B2
    B2 -->|"Staff reading r (downward)"| B["Ground Point B"]
    B1 -->|"Vertical distance s tan theta + r"| B
