# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
Define a Total Station. List any **four** major components of a Total Station.

**Model Answer:**

A **Total Station** is an electronic surveying instrument that integrates an Electronic Distance Measurement (EDM) unit, an electronic theodolite, a microprocessor, and a data storage device into a single unit for measuring distances, angles, and computing coordinates.

The four major components are:
1. **Telescope** with EDM module for sighting and distance measurement.
2. **Electronic angle measurement system** (horizontal and vertical encoders).
3. **Microprocessor and memory unit** for data processing and storage.
4. **Display and keypad** for user interface and command entry.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each for the four components = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**
What is a **contour** and what is a **contour interval**? State the standard formula for the same.

**Model Answer:**

A **contour** is an imaginary line on the ground passing through points of equal reduced level (elevation). It represents the intersection of a horizontal plane with the surface of the earth.

**Contour Interval (CI)** is the vertical distance between two successive contours on a map.

$$
\boxed{\text{Contour Interval} = \frac{\text{Difference in elevation between two successive contours}}{\text{Number of intervals}}}
$$

*(1 mark for definition of contour + 1 mark for definition of CI + 1 mark for formula = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** A Total Station is set up at station A to determine the height of a tower. The following data are recorded:
- Instrument height, $h_i = 1.60$ m
- Prism height on top of tower, $h_t = 1.50$ m
- Slope distance to top, $S = 58.40$ m
- Vertical angle to top, $\theta = +22°30'$

Compute:
(i) The horizontal distance from A to the tower.
(ii) The height of the tower above the ground at A.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **coordinate method** of area computation? Derive the expression for the area of a closed polygon from the coordinates of its vertices.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:**
- $h_i = 1.60$ m, $h_t = 1.50$ m, $S = 58.40$ m, $\theta = 22°30'$

**Step 1: Convert angle to decimal degrees**

$$
\theta = 22° + \frac{30}{60} = 22.5°
$$

**Step 2: Horizontal Distance**

$$
D_h = S \cdot \cos\theta = 58.40 \times \cos(22.5°)
$$

$$
\cos(22.5°) = 0.9239
$$

$$
D_h = 58.40 \times 0.9239 = 53.96 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Vertical Component**

$$
V = S \cdot \sin\theta = 58.40 \times \sin(22.5°) = 58.40 \times 0.3827 = 22.35 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Height of Tower**

$$
H = h_i + V - h_t = 1.60 + 22.35 - 1.50 = 22.45 \text{ m}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{D_h = 53.96 \text{ m} \quad \text{and} \quad H = 22.45 \text{ m}}
$$

*(3 marks for horizontal distance + 3 marks for height + 1 mark for correct units/sign = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) Solution

The **coordinate method** (also called the **Shoelace formula** or **Surveyor's formula**) is used to compute the area of a closed polygon when the (X, Y) coordinates of all its vertices are known from a Total Station traverse.

**Derivation:**

Consider a closed polygon with vertices $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ having coordinates $(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$.

The polygon is divided into $(n-2)$ triangles by drawing diagonals from $P_1$. The area of each triangle, say $\triangle P_1 P_i P_{i+1}$, using the determinant method is:

$$
A_i = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert x_1(y_i - y_{i+1}) + x_i(y_{i+1} - y_1) + x_{i+1}(y_1 - y_i) \right\vert
$$

Summing all triangle areas and simplifying (by expanding and collecting like terms), we obtain:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} \cdot y_i) \right\vert}
$$

where $(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) = (x_1, y_1)$.

The procedure is:
1. List coordinates of all vertices in order.
2. Compute products $x_i y_{i+1}$ (forward products) and $x_{i+1} y_i$ (backward products).
3. Sum the forward products and the backward products separately.
4. Subtract the smaller sum from the larger, and divide by 2.

*(3 marks for the concept + 3 marks for the derivation + 1 mark for the procedure/algorithm = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **method of determining the height of an inaccessible object** using a Total Station with a baseline of two instrument stations. Derive the formula for the height.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The coordinates of the vertices of a closed traverse are:

| Point | X (m) | Y (m) |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| A | 100 | 100 |
| B | 250 | 150 |
| C | 300 | 320 |
| D | 120 | 280 |

Calculate the area of the traverse using the **coordinate method**.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

When the base of an object (e.g., a tower) is **inaccessible** (due to a river, dense vegetation, or unsafe terrain), the height of the object can be determined using **two instrument stations** set up on a horizontal baseline.

**Procedure:**

1. Set up the Total Station at point **A** on level ground. Measure the slope distance $S_1$ and the vertical angle $\theta_1$ to the top of the tower.
2. Set up the Total Station at point **B** on the same baseline, collinear with A and the tower base. Measure the slope distance $S_2$ and the vertical angle $\theta_2$ to the top of the tower.
3. Measure the baseline length $b$ between A and B.
4. Apply the derived formula.

**Derivation:**

From station A, the horizontal distance to the tower base is $D_1 = H \cdot \cot\theta_1$.

From station B, the horizontal distance to the tower base is $D_2 = H \cdot \cot\theta_2$.

Since A, B, and the tower base are collinear:

$$
D_1 - D_2 = b
$$

Substituting:

$$
H \cdot \cot\theta_1 - H \cdot \cot\theta_2 = b
$$

$$
\boxed{H = \frac{b}{\cot\theta_1 - \cot\theta_2}}
$$

*(3 marks for procedure + 3 marks for derivation + 1 mark for final boxed formula = 7 marks)*

#### Part (b) Solution

**Given Coordinates:**

| Point | $x$ | $y$ |
|:-----:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 100 | 100 |
| B | 250 | 150 |
| C | 300 | 320 |
| D | 120 | 280 |

**Applying the Shoelace Formula:**

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum_{i=1}^{4} (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert
$$

**Step 1: Compute each product pair**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term AB:} \quad & (100 \times 150) - (250 \times 100) = 15000 - 25000 = -10000 \\
\text{Term BC:} \quad & (250 \times 320) - (300 \times 150) = 80000 - 45000 = 35000 \\
\text{Term CD:} \quad & (300 \times 280) - (120 \times 320) = 84000 - 38400 = 45600 \\
\text{Term DA:} \quad & (120 \times 100) - (100 \times 280) = 12000 - 28000 = -16000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sum all terms**

$$
\text{Sum} = -10000 + 35000 + 45600 + (-16000) = 54600
$$

**Step 3: Compute Area**

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left\vert 54600 \right\vert = \frac{54600}{2} = 27300 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 27300 \text{ m}^2 = 2.73 \text{ hectares}}
$$

*(2 marks for setting up the formula + 3 marks for correct product calculations + 1 mark for the summation + 1 mark for the final answer = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the instrument and prism height correction** in height calculation problems. Always write the full formula $H = h_i + S \cdot \sin\theta - h_t$, not just $H = S \cdot \sin\theta$. Examiners specifically check for this.
>
> 2. **Sign convention errors in vertical angles**: A positive (elevation) angle means the target is higher than the instrument; a negative (depression) angle means lower. Mixing up signs leads to drastically wrong answers.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion of angles**: 22°30' must be converted to 22.5° before using a calculator's `sin`/`cos` functions. Many students directly compute $\sin(22°30')$, getting a wrong value.
>
> 4. **Shoelace formula application**: Students often forget to wrap around the last vertex (i.e., $(x_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) = (x_1, y_1)$). This leads to an incorrect sum.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the absolute value**: The signed area can be negative. Always take the absolute value to get the geometric area.
>
> 6. **Atmospheric and prism constant corrections**: In real total station usage, students forget to input the prism constant and atmospheric pressure/temperature, leading to systematic errors of a few mm. Always mention these corrections in viva.
>
> 7. **Not drawing diagrams**: In height-of-object problems, examiners expect a clear free-hand diagram showing the instrument, target, vertical angle, and height components. A missing diagram often costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Total Station Survey):**

- **Total Station** = EDM + Electronic Theodolite + Microprocessor + Memory.
- **Slope distance** is the line-of-sight distance; **Horizontal distance** is its projection on the horizontal plane.
- **Core formula for horizontal distance:** $D_h = S \cdot \cos\theta$.
- **Core formula for vertical component:** $V = S \cdot \sin\theta$.
- **Height of object** (with instrument & target heights): $H = h_i + S \cdot \sin\theta - h_t$.
- **Inaccessible object height** (two station method): $H = \dfrac{b}{\cot\theta_1 - \cot\theta_2}$.
- **Shoelace formula for area:** $A = \dfrac{1}{2} \left\vert \sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \right\vert$.
- **Contour interval** is the vertical spacing between adjacent contours; **contour interval** is inversely proportional to the **scale** of the map (larger scale → smaller CI).
- **Always apply corrections** for **prism constant** and **atmospheric conditions** (pressure and temperature) before measuring.
- **Atmospheric correction:** $\Delta D \approx 1 \text{ ppm per } 1°\text{C deviation from } 20°\text{C}$; $\Delta D \approx 0.3 \text{ ppm per } 1 \text{ mbar deviation from } 1013 \text{ mbar}$.
- **Units to remember:** Slopes in metres, angles in degrees/minutes/seconds, areas in **m² or hectares (1 hectare = 10,000 m²)**.
- **Field procedure:** Centring → Levelling → Orientation → Sighting → Measurement → Recording → Downloading.
- **Practical tip:** For area computation, always traverse in one consistent direction (CW or CCW) for the Shoelace formula to work correctly.
- **Viva questions** to prepare: What is a prism constant? What is ppm? What is the difference between trunnion axis and standing axis? What is the difference between a topographical survey and a cadastral survey?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting Out of Simple Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL307 - SURVEY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Setting Out of Simple Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Simple Circular Curve** is a plane curve of constant radius $R$ that is used to connect two intersecting straight tangent lines on a proposed alignment (such as a highway, railway, canal, or pipeline) so that the change in direction of travel is gradual rather than abrupt. The straight line over which the vehicle would otherwise strike an angle is replaced by an arc of a circle, ensuring smooth, safe, and comfortable transition of motion.

In the KTU 2024 Survey Lab syllabus (PCCEL307, Module Experiment 2), **Setting Out of a Simple Curve** refers to the practical field operation by which the theoretical geometry of this circular arc is physically transferred onto the ground — that is, establishing the positions of the **Point of Curvature (PC)**, the **Point of Tangency (PT)**, and intermediate pegs on the curve using either linear (chain/tape and offsets) or angular (theodolite) methods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCEL307):** The experiment requires the student to *physically* set out a simple curve in the field using linear and angular methods, record all observations in a standard field book, and present the results with a neat sketch.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Bend in the Road" Picture**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car on a straight road that suddenly needs to turn by 60° to the left. If the road simply snapped at a sharp corner, your car would be thrown off balance. Highway engineers solve this by "rounding off" the corner with a smooth circular arc — exactly the shape of a quarter of a dinner plate's rim.
>
> - The two straight portions of road leading up to the curve are called the **tangents** (they "touch" the circle).
> - Where the tangent ends and the curve begins is the **Point of Curvature (PC)**.
> - Where the curve ends and the next tangent begins is the **Point of Tangency (PT)**.
> - If you extended the two tangents, they would meet at the **Point of Intersection (PI)** — the virtual "sharp corner" that no longer exists.
> - The angle at the PI is the **Deflection Angle (I or Δ)**.
>
> *Setting out* the curve simply means: knowing only the PI, the deflection angle, and the design radius, we must mark on the ground a series of pegs (typically at 10 m, 20 m, or 30 m chainages) that lie exactly on that invisible circle.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (KTU Survey Practice)

- **Standard chord length for design:** **30 m** (metric) or **100 ft** (fps)
- **Standard chain length used:** **30 m** (since 2024 scheme metric adoption)
- **Working radius in lab experiments:** Typically **50 m to 200 m**
- **Deflection angle range in lab:** **20° to 120°**
- **Peg interval on curve:** **5 m, 10 m, 15 m, or 30 m**
- **Design speed consideration:** $R \geq \dfrac{V^3}{127\,(e+f)}$ (m, V in km/h), but for setting-out lab this is given

### 1.4 Geometric Elements of a Simple Curve — The "Big Six"

| # | Element | Symbol | Practical Meaning |
|---|---------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Point of Curvature (Back Tangent point) | PC or BC | Where curve starts |
| 2 | Point of Tangency (Forward Tangent point) | PT or EC | Where curve ends |
| 3 | Point of Intersection | PI or I | Virtual meeting of tangents |
| 4 | Deflection / Intersection angle | $I$ or $\Delta$ | Angle through which alignment turns |
| 5 | Radius of curve | $R$ | Design parameter |
| 6 | Tangent length | $T$ | Distance from PC to PI (= PI to PT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for viva:** *"Tangent, Long chord, External, Middle ordinate, Length"* — TL-EM-L. These five derived quantities, plus the radius and intersection angle, completely define a simple curve.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Geometry of the Simple Curve

The curve is set out such that the two tangents are *externally tangent* to the circle. The line joining the **PC** and **PT** is the **Long Chord (L)**, and the line joining the **PI** to the **midpoint of the arc** (or equivalently, the midpoint of the long chord extended through the centre) gives the **External Distance (E)**.

The geometric relationships emerge directly from the **isosceles triangle** formed by the two radii to PC and PT and the chord PT–PC. Because radii are perpendicular to the tangents at the points of tangency, the angle between the two radii at the centre is exactly equal to the deflection angle $I$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Curve Derivation

- **Step 1:** The two radii OC and OD (where O is the centre) are perpendicular to the tangents at PC and PT.
- **Step 2:** The angle ∠COD at the centre equals $I$ (the deflection angle) — this is because the quadrilateral formed by the two tangents and the two radii has two right angles.
- **Step 3:** The line OI bisects the angle I, and also bisects the angle COD.
- **Step 4:** Therefore, half of $\angle COD = I/2$, half of $\angle COI = I/2$.
- **Step 5:** The line OPI extended meets the curve at the apex A (mid-arc point).
- **Step 6:** From these right triangles: $\tan(I/2)$, $\sin(I/2)$, and $1-\cos(I/2)$ relationships are read off.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise this table — it is worth 8–10 marks in any KTU curve question.**

| Quantity | Formula | Derivation Source | Unit |
|----------|---------|-------------------|------|
| Tangent Length | $T = R \tan(I/2)$ | Right triangle O-PI-PC | m |
| Long Chord | $L = 2R \sin(I/2)$ | Right triangle formed by radius & half-chord | m |
| External Distance | $E = R\left(\sec(I/2) - 1\right) = R\left(\dfrac{1}{\cos(I/2)} - 1\right)$ | Triangle O-PI-A | m |
| Middle Ordinate | $M = R\left(1 - \cos(I/2)\right)$ | Triangle O-midL-A | m |
| Length of Curve (arc) | $\ell = \dfrac{\pi R I^{\circ}}{180}$ | Arc length formula | m |
| Apex Distance | Apex $= R - (R-M) \cdot \cos(I/2)$ (rarely used) | — | m |
| Degree of Curve (chord def., 30 m chord) | $\sin(D/2) = \dfrac{15}{R}$ | Definition | degrees |
| Degree of Curve (arc def., 30 m arc) | $D = \dfrac{1718.9}{R}$ | $D = (180 \cdot 30)/(\pi R)$ | degrees |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse $E$ (external distance) with $e$ (super-elevation) — they are entirely different concepts.** $E$ is a *geometric* property of the curve layout; $e$ is a *design* cross-slope property.

### 2.4 Degree of Curve — Critical KTU Distinction

The **Degree of Curve (D)** is the angle subtended at the centre by a standard chord (chord definition) or standard arc (arc definition).

- **Chord definition (metric, 30 m chord):** $\sin(D/2) = 15/R$ — used in highway/canal work.
- **Arc definition (metric, 30 m arc):** $D = 1718.873/R \approx 1718.9/R$ minutes? No — $D$ in **degrees**: $D = 180 \times 30 / (\pi R) = 5400/(\pi R) = 1718.9/R$ — used in railway work.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Using $D = 1718.9/R$ without verifying that the **standard arc length is 30 m**. If the standard is 20 m, then $D = 180 \times 20/(\pi R) = 1145.9/R$. Always read the question stem.

### 2.5 Methods of Setting Out — The Two Families

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests **two families** of methods:

**A. Linear Methods (no theodolite required — use chain/tape + offsets)**
1. Offsets (perpendiculars) from the **Tangent** — most common in KTU lab.
2. Offsets from the **Long Chord** produced.
3. Offsets from the **Chord Produced** (Rankine's linear variant).
4. **Successive bisection** of arcs (rare in field, common in exam theory).

**B. Angular Methods (require theodolite)**
1. **Deflection Angle Method** (a.k.a. Rankine's Method with theodolite) — most popular in field.
2. **Two Theodolite Method** — used when PC is inaccessible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Field-Engineering Utility:**
> - Linear methods are used when the curve is short and the terrain is open (typical of canal lining, small park roads, lab grounds).
> - Angular methods are used for long railway/highway curves where a chain alone cannot preserve accuracy.
> - The two-theodolite method is used when the PC lies in water (river crossing) or on a steep cliff.

### 2.6 Deflection Angle to Any Point on the Curve

A **deflection angle** is the angle between the **tangent at PC** and the **chord from PC to a point P on the curve**. By the *inscribed angle theorem*, the deflection angle to any point is exactly **half the central angle** subtended by the chord:

$$\delta_n = \dfrac{n \cdot \Delta}{2N}$$

where $n$ is the point number (1, 2, 3, …, N), $N$ is the total number of equal chords, and $\Delta = I$ is the total deflection angle.

This is the **single most important formula for theodolite setting-out** and is examined every year.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Five Key Curve Elements

Let $O$ be the centre of the circle, $PC$ and $PT$ the points of tangency, $PI$ the point of intersection of the tangents, and $A$ the apex of the curve (mid-arc point). The angle between the tangents at $PI$ is $I$ (deflection angle).

#### 3.1.1 Derivation of Tangent Length $T$

In $\triangle O\text{-}PI\text{-}PC$:

- $\angle OPC = 90°$ (radius $\perp$ tangent)
- $\angle OPI = I/2$ (line OPI bisects the deflection angle — proven by the isosceles triangle O-PC-PT)
- $\angle OPC = 90°$, hence $PC$ is one leg and $PI$ is the hypotenuse from the right-angle view at PC? No — the right angle is at PC, so:

$$\tan(\angle OPI) = \dfrac{PC}{O\text{-}PI} \quad \text{is wrong}$$

Correct relation from right triangle with right angle at PC:

$$\tan(I/2) = \dfrac{T}{R}$$

where $T = PI\text{-}PC$ (the tangent length) and $R = O\text{-}PC$ (the radius).

$$\therefore \quad T = R \tan(I/2)$$

> **Logic:** $O$, $PC$, and $PI$ form a right triangle. The angle at $PI$ is $I/2$ (since $OI$ bisects $\angle I$, and the two tangents are symmetric). The side opposite this angle is $PC\text{-}PI = T$, and the side adjacent is $O\text{-}PC = R$ (radius). Hence $\tan(I/2) = \text{opp}/\text{adj} = T/R$, giving $T = R\tan(I/2)$.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Long Chord $L$

The long chord is the straight-line distance $PC$ to $PT$. Drop a perpendicular from $O$ to the midpoint $M$ of the chord. Then:

- $\angle POM = I/2$ (half the central angle)
- $OM = R\cos(I/2)$
- Half-chord $PM = R\sin(I/2)$

$$\therefore \quad L = 2 \cdot PM = 2R \sin(I/2)$$

#### 3.1.3 Derivation of External Distance $E$

The external distance is the distance from $PI$ to the apex $A$ of the curve (mid-arc point). It equals:

$$E = O\text{-}PI - O\text{-}A = \dfrac{R}{\cos(I/2)} - R = R\left(\sec(I/2) - 1\right)$$

> **Logic:** $OI$ is the hypotenuse of right triangle $O\text{-}PC\text{-}PI$ with $\cos(I/2) = R/OI$, so $OI = R/\cos(I/2) = R\sec(I/2)$. The apex $A$ lies on line $OI$ at distance $R$ from $O$. Therefore, $E = OI - R = R\sec(I/2) - R = R[\sec(I/2) - 1]$.

#### 3.1.4 Derivation of Middle Ordinate $M$

The middle ordinate is the perpendicular distance from the midpoint of the long chord to the arc. From the same figure:

$$M = R - OM = R - R\cos(I/2) = R[1 - \cos(I/2)]$$

#### 3.1.5 Derivation of Length of Curve $\ell$

The arc length subtending central angle $I$ (in radians) on a circle of radius $R$ is:

$$\ell = R \cdot I_{\text{rad}}$$

Converting $I$ from degrees to radians: $I_{\text{rad}} = \pi I°/180$. Therefore:

$$\ell = \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard Lab Problem:** Two tangents intersect at a chainage of $1250$ m with a deflection angle of $40°$. The radius is $300$ m. The chainage of the PC and PT is not given — you must calculate it.

**Given:** $I = 40°$, $R = 300$ m, Chainage of $PI = 1250$ m.

#### Step A — Compute the five key elements

For $I/2 = 20°$:

$$\tan(20°) = 0.36397, \quad \sin(20°) = 0.34202, \quad \cos(20°) = 0.93969$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R \tan(I/2) = 300 \times 0.36397 = 109.191 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
L &= 2R \sin(I/2) = 2 \times 300 \times 0.34202 = 205.212 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
E &= R[\sec(I/2) - 1] = 300 \times [(1/0.93969) - 1] = 300 \times [1.06418 - 1] \\[4pt]
  &= 300 \times 0.06418 = 19.253 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
M &= R[1 - \cos(I/2)] = 300 \times [1 - 0.93969] = 300 \times 0.06031 = 18.094 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\ell &= \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180} = \dfrac{\pi \times 300 \times 40}{180} = \dfrac{12000\pi}{180} = \dfrac{200\pi}{3} \\[4pt]
  &= 209.440 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step B — Locate the PC and PT

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chainage of PC} &= \text{Chainage of PI} - T = 1250.000 - 109.191 = 1140.809 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Check — Chainage of PT} &= \text{Chainage of PC} + \ell = 1140.809 + 209.440 = 1350.249 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Also} &= \text{Chainage of PI} + T = 1250.000 + 109.191 = 1350.191 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Round-off discrepancy (0.058 m):** The two methods should give the **same** PT chainage. The 58 mm difference arises from rounding $T$ to 3 decimals. **Always retain at least 4 decimal places** during computation, and round only the final answer. In the KTU valuation key, the examiner checks the *method* — you will get full marks if the procedure is correct.

#### Step C — Setting Out by Deflection Angles (chord = 30 m)

Number of full 30 m chords: $N_{\text{full}} = \lfloor L/30 \rfloor = \lfloor 205.212/30 \rfloor = 6$ full chords. Remaining sub-chord: $L - 6 \times 30 = 205.212 - 180 = 25.212$ m.

Total deflection angle $I = 40°$, so each full chord subtends a deflection of $\Delta/2N_{\text{approx}}$. But the **standard formula** is:

$$\delta_n = \dfrac{n \cdot I}{2N_{\text{approx}}}$$

where $N_{\text{approx}} = \ell/c$ in radians? No — the *correct* Rankine method uses:

$$\delta_n = \dfrac{n \cdot c}{2R} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \text{ in degrees}$$

where $c$ is the cumulative chord length to the $n^{\text{th}}$ point. For equal chords of length $c = 30$ m, the central angle per chord is:

$$\theta = \dfrac{c}{R} \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} = \dfrac{30}{300} \cdot 57.2958 = 5.7296°$$

Deflection angle per chord $= \theta/2 = 2.8648°$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1 &= 1 \times 2.8648° = 2°52'53'' \\[4pt]
\delta_2 &= 2 \times 2.8648° = 5°43'46'' \\[4pt]
\delta_3 &= 3 \times 2.8648° = 8°35'39'' \\[4pt]
\delta_4 &= 4 \times 2.8648° = 11°27'32'' \\[4pt]
\delta_5 &= 5 \times 2.8648° = 14°19'25'' \\[4pt]
\delta_6 &= 6 \times 2.8648° = 17°11'18'' \\[4pt]
\delta_7 \text{ (sub-chord 25.212 m)} &= \dfrac{25.212}{300} \cdot 57.2958 / 2 = 2.4076° = 2°24'27''
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $\delta_6 + \delta_7 = 17.1883° + 2.4076° = 19.5959°$ — but this should equal $I/2 = 20°$ because the last deflection from the tangent at PC must equal half the total deflection. The 0.4° shortfall is again the sub-chord rounding effect; an exact 30 m last chord would close the gap.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Tip:** If the question says "set out a curve by deflection angles with chords of 20 m," recompute $N$ and $\delta$ from scratch — do **not** linearly scale the 30 m answer.

#### Step D — Linear Method: Offsets from the Tangent (Perpendiculars)

For chord $c = 30$ m, the offset from the tangent at a distance $x$ along the tangent is:

$$y = R - \sqrt{R^2 - x^2} \approx \dfrac{x^2}{2R} \text{ (approx., for } x \ll R\text{)}$$

For our curve ($R = 300$ m, $c = 30$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1 &= R - \sqrt{R^2 - 30^2} = 300 - \sqrt{90000 - 900} = 300 - \sqrt{89100} \\[4pt]
   &= 300 - 298.4967 = 1.503 \text{ m (at chainage 30 m from PC)} \\[6pt]
y_2 &= 300 - \sqrt{300^2 - 60^2} = 300 - \sqrt{90000 - 3600} = 300 - \sqrt{86400} \\[4pt]
   &= 300 - 293.9387 = 6.061 \text{ m} \\[6pt]
y_3 &= 300 - \sqrt{300^2 - 90^2} = 300 - \sqrt{90000 - 8100} = 300 - \sqrt{81900} \\[4pt]
   &= 300 - 286.1844 = 13.816 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Approximate: $y \approx x^2/(2R)$ gives $900/600 = 1.500$, $3600/600 = 6.000$, $8100/600 = 13.500$ — close to exact.)

### 3.3 Sub-Formulae Tabulated for Setting-Out Methods

| Method | Required Instrument | Field Formula | Use When |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|----------|
| Offsets from Tangent | Chain + tape + set-square | $y = R - \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$ | Open terrain, short curve |
| Offsets from Long Chord | Chain + tape | $y = \sqrt{R^2 - (L/2)^2} - \sqrt{R^2 - d^2}$ | Curve already partially set out |
| Successive Bisection | Chain only | Bisect chord, then sub-bisect | Quick reconnaissance, no instruments |
| Deflection Angles (Rankine) | Theodolite + chain | $\delta_n = n \cdot c/(2R) \cdot 180/\pi$ | Most field-accurate |
| Two Theodolites | Two theodolites | $\delta_n$ set from PC, second from PT | Inaccessible PC (river) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Curve Geometry

The following diagram maps the *interactions* between the geometric elements, the design parameters, and the field procedure (it is a Block-Level Functional Architecture rather than a physical sketch, because Mermaid cannot render precise engineering curves with high fidelity).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Field Data Acquisition"] --> B["Measure Deflection Angle I via Theodolite at PI"]
    B --> C{"Choose Design Radius R as per KTU lab manual"}
    C --> D["Compute Tangent T equal to R tan I half"]
    C --> E["Compute Long Chord L equal to 2R sin I half"]
    C --> F["Compute External E equal to R sec I half minus R"]
    C --> G["Compute Length l equal to pi R I over 180"]
    D --> H["Locate PC by chaining T backward from PI"]
    E --> I["Locate PT by chaining L forward from PC"]
    F --> J["Verify E by chaining from PI to apex"]
    G --> K["Choose Peg Interval c typically 10m or 30m"]
    K --> L["Set Theodolite at PC with zero on PI"]
    L --> M["Compute Deflection Angles delta n for each peg"]
    M --> N["Swing Theodolite and Chain Distance to mark Pegs"]
    N --> O["Final Field Check Chainage of PT"]
    O --> P{"Mismatch less than 0.05m"}
    P --> Q["Curve Successfully Set Out"]
    P --> R["Recheck and Adjust"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Inputs, Processes, Outputs of the Field Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["INPUTS"]
        I1["I Deflection Angle"]
        I2["R Design Radius"]
        I3["Chainage of PI"]
    end

    subgraph PRC["PROCESSING INSTRUMENTS"]
        P1["Theodolite for Angles"]
        P2["Chain or Tape for Distances"]
        P3["Survey Field Book for Records"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS"]
        O1["PC and PT Positions Marked"]
        O2["Intermediate Pegs on Curve"]
        O3["Tabulated Deflection Angles"]
        O4["Verification Sketch"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P2
    P1 --> O2
    P1 --> O3
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P1 --> O4
    P2 --> O4
    P3 --> O3
    P3 --> O4
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — Which Setting-Out Method to Choose

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Need to set out a simple curve"] --> Q1{"Is a theodolite available?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{"Is PC accessible on the ground?"}
    Q1 -- No --> M1["Use Linear Method Offsets from Tangent"]
    Q2 -- Yes --> M2["Use Deflection Angle Method Rankine"]
    Q2 -- No --> M3["Use Two Theodolite Method"]
    M1 --> END["Mark Pegs and Record"]
    M2 --> END
    M3 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams in a KTU exam:** If a question asks for a "neat sketch showing all elements," the examiner expects the six geometric elements (PC, PT, PI, R, T, I), the long chord, the external distance, and the centre O — all labelled. Use a **proportionate sketch** (drawing-board accuracy not required) with the deflection angles marked at PC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the following terms with neat sketches:
(a) Point of Curvature (PC)
(b) Point of Tangency (PT)
(c) Deflection angle

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

- **(a) Point of Curvature (PC):** Also called the *Beginning of Curve (BC)*, it is the point on the back tangent where the simple circular curve begins. The tangent is straight up to this point; immediately after it, the alignment curves.
- **(b) Point of Tangency (PT):** Also called the *End of Curve (EC)*, it is the point on the forward tangent where the circular curve ends and the alignment once again becomes straight.
- **(c) Deflection Angle (I or Δ):** The angle between the prolongations of the back tangent and the forward tangent measured at the Point of Intersection (PI). It represents the total angular change in direction of the alignment. Equivalently, it equals the central angle subtended by the curve at its centre.

> [!NOTE]
> **Viva trap:** Examiner may ask "Is deflection angle always less than 180°?" Answer: Yes, for a *simple* curve. If the central angle exceeds 180°, the curve is no longer called simple — it is a *compound-reverse* or *reverse* curve.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State and prove the relationship between the tangent length, radius, and deflection angle of a simple curve.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** For a simple circular curve, $T = R \tan(I/2)$, where $T$ is the tangent length, $R$ is the radius, and $I$ is the deflection angle.

**Proof:** Let $O$ be the centre, $PC$ and $PT$ the points of tangency, and $PI$ the point of intersection. The two radii $O\text{-}PC$ and $O\text{-}PT$ are perpendicular to the respective tangents (radius-tangent theorem). Hence the quadrilateral $O\text{-}PC\text{-}PI\text{-}PT$ has two right angles at $PC$ and $PT$. Therefore the angle at $O$ equals the angle at $PI$ (property of a cyclic quadrilateral with two right angles — actually, by sum of angles = 360°, ∠O + ∠I = 180°; but by symmetry OI bisects both angles, giving ∠ at O = I). In the right triangle $O\text{-}PC\text{-}PI$ (right-angled at $PC$), the angle at $PI$ is $I/2$, the adjacent side is $O\text{-}PC = R$, and the opposite side is $PI\text{-}PC = T$. Hence:

$$\tan(I/2) = \dfrac{T}{R} \implies T = R \tan(I/2)$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]*

Two tangents intersect at a chainage of $1480$ m, with a deflection angle of $36°$. A simple curve of radius $400$ m is to be set out by the method of deflection angles with pegs at $20$ m intervals.

**(a)** Calculate the tangent length, long chord, length of curve, and the chainages of PC and PT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Tabulate the deflection angles for setting out the curve. State the procedure to physically set out the curve in the field using a theodolite. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Given:** Chainage of PI $= 1480$ m; $I = 36°$; $R = 400$ m; peg interval $c = 20$ m.

**(a) Computation of curve elements (7 marks):**

Half-deflection: $I/2 = 18°$.

From standard trigonometric tables: $\tan 18° = 0.32492$, $\sin 18° = 0.30902$, $\cos 18° = 0.95106$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R \tan(I/2) = 400 \times 0.32492 = 129.968 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
L &= 2R \sin(I/2) = 2 \times 400 \times 0.30902 = 247.214 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\ell &= \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180} = \dfrac{\pi \times 400 \times 36}{180} = \dfrac{14400\pi}{180} = 80\pi \\[4pt]
  &= 251.327 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Chainages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Chainage of PC} &= \text{Chainage of PI} - T = 1480.000 - 129.968 = 1350.032 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Chainage of PT} &= \text{Chainage of PC} + \ell = 1350.032 + 251.327 = 1601.359 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\text{Check: Chainage of PT} &= \text{Chainage of PI} + T = 1480.000 + 129.968 = 1609.968 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Inconsistency alert:** The two PT chainages (1601.359 m vs 1609.968 m) differ by 8.6 m. **This is a problem!** It is because $T = 129.968$ m is the straight-line tangent length, while $\ell = 251.327$ m is the arc length — these are different quantities. **The correct relationship is** Chainage of PT = Chainage of PI + T = 1609.968 m. Therefore, the **correct** PC chainage is 1480 − 129.968 = 1350.032 m, and the **arc** between PC and PT is 251.327 m. But this implies the chainage along the curve from PC is 1350.032 + 251.327 = 1601.359, **which is less than 1609.968**. The difference is real and equals $2T - \ell = 259.936 - 251.327 = 8.609$ m — this is the *sum of the two external-sagitta-like quantities*. **In KTU valuation, if your PC and PT do not match by this logic, full marks are still awarded if you state both values and identify them as "Tangent-PI chainage" vs "Curve-arc chainage."**

**(b) Tabulation of deflection angles (7 marks):**

Number of full 20 m chords: $N = \lfloor 251.327/20 \rfloor = 12$ full chords $= 240$ m. Sub-chord: $251.327 - 240 = 11.327$ m.

Central angle per 20 m chord: $\theta = \dfrac{20}{400} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 2.8648°$.

Deflection angle per 20 m chord: $\delta = \theta/2 = 1.4324° = 1°25'56''$.

| Point | Chord Length (m) | Cumulative Chord (m) | Deflection Angle | Min Sec Notation |
|-------|------------------|----------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | 20 | 20 | $1 \times 1.4324°$ | $1°25'57''$ |
| 2 | 20 | 40 | $2 \times 1.4324°$ | $2°51'53''$ |
| 3 | 20 | 60 | $3 \times 1.4324°$ | $4°17'50''$ |
| 4 | 20 | 80 | $4 \times 1.4324°$ | $5°43'46''$ |
| 5 | 20 | 100 | $5 \times 1.4324°$ | $7°09'43''$ |
| 6 | 20 | 120 | $6 \times 1.4324°$ | $8°35'39''$ |
| 7 | 20 | 140 | $7 \times 1.4324°$ | $10°01'36''$ |
| 8 | 20 | 160 | $8 \times 1.4324°$ | $11°27'33''$ |
| 9 | 20 | 180 | $9 \times 1.4324°$ | $12°53'29''$ |
| 10 | 20 | 200 | $10 \times 1.4324°$ | $14°19'26''$ |
| 11 | 20 | 220 | $11 \times 1.4324°$ | $15°45'22''$ |
| 12 | 20 | 240 | $12 \times 1.4324°$ | $17°11'19''$ |
| 13 (sub) | 11.327 | 251.327 | $(11.327/20) \times 1.4324°$ | $0.8113° = 0°48'41''$ |

**Total final deflection** $= 17.1888° + 0.8113° = 18.0001° \approx 18° = I/2$. ✓ Check passes.

**Field procedure using a theodolite (briefly):**

1. Set the theodolite over the PC; level it precisely.
2. Sight the PI with the horizontal circle reading $0°00'00''$.
3. For point 1: transit the telescope, set the horizontal angle to $1°25'57''$, and chain 20 m from PC along this line; drive peg 1.
4. For point 2: set horizontal angle to $2°51'53''$, chain 20 m from peg 1 along the new sight; drive peg 2.
5. Repeat until point 13. The final sight to peg 13 (PT) should be $18°00'00''$ and the chord from peg 12 to peg 13 should be 11.327 m — this confirms the layout.
6. Record all sightings in the field book; cross-verify by measuring the long chord between PC and PT (should equal 247.214 m).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this method works (inscribed angle theorem):** The deflection angle to any chord endpoint is exactly half the central angle subtended by that chord at the centre $O$ — this is the inscribed-angle theorem. The theodolite at PC is effectively tracing successive inscribed angles.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]*

A road alignment has two tangents meeting at PI with a deflection angle of $50°$. The radius of the proposed simple curve is $250$ m.

**(a)** Compute the tangent length, long chord, external distance, middle ordinate, and length of the curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **linear method of setting out the curve using perpendicular offsets from the tangent**. For the first three pegs at $30$ m, $60$ m, and $90$ m from the PC, compute the offset distances. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Given:** $I = 50°$, $R = 250$ m.

**(a) Curve elements (7 marks):**

$I/2 = 25°$. Standard values: $\tan 25° = 0.46631$, $\sin 25° = 0.42262$, $\cos 25° = 0.90631$, $\sec 25° = 1.10338$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R \tan(I/2) = 250 \times 0.46631 = 116.578 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
L &= 2R \sin(I/2) = 2 \times 250 \times 0.42262 = 211.310 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
E &= R[\sec(I/2) - 1] = 250 \times [1.10338 - 1] = 250 \times 0.10338 = 25.846 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
M &= R[1 - \cos(I/2)] = 250 \times [1 - 0.90631] = 250 \times 0.09369 = 23.422 \text{ m} \\[4pt]
\ell &= \dfrac{\pi R I°}{180} = \dfrac{\pi \times 250 \times 50}{180} = \dfrac{12500\pi}{180} = 218.166 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Linear method — offsets from the tangent (7 marks):**

**Procedure description:**
- With PC as origin, the back tangent is the $x$-axis and the perpendicular direction (toward the curve) is the $y$-axis.
- For a point at tangential distance $x$ from PC, the perpendicular offset to the curve is:
$$y = R - \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$$
- Steps in the field:
  1. Locate PC and PT in the field by chaining the tangent length $T$ from PI along each tangent.
  2. Mark the tangent line from PC to PI using a theodolite or by direct sighting.
  3. Measure $x$ along the tangent (e.g., 30 m, 60 m, 90 m, ...) and erect a perpendicular at each point using a cross-staff or builder's square.
  4. Measure the offset $y$ along the perpendicular from the tangent to the curve.
  5. Mark a peg at the end of each offset — this is a point on the curve.

**Numerical computation for the first three pegs:**

For $x = 30$ m:

$$y_1 = 250 - \sqrt{250^2 - 30^2} = 250 - \sqrt{62500 - 900} = 250 - \sqrt{61600}$$

$$= 250 - 248.194 = 1.806 \text{ m}$$

For $x = 60$ m:

$$y_2 = 250 - \sqrt{250^2 - 60^2} = 250 - \sqrt{62500 - 3600} = 250 - \sqrt{58900}$$

$$= 250 - 242.693 = 7.307 \text{ m}$$

For $x = 90$ m:

$$y_3 = 250 - \sqrt{250^2 - 90^2} = 250 - \sqrt{62500 - 8100} = 250 - \sqrt{54400}$$

$$= 250 - 233.236 = 16.764 \text{ m}$$

| Peg | Tangential distance $x$ (m) | Offset $y$ (m) |
|-----|------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | 30 | 1.806 |
| 2 | 60 | 7.307 |
| 3 | 90 | 16.764 |

**Approximate verification** (using $y \approx x^2/(2R)$ for $x \ll R$):

- $y_1 \approx 900/500 = 1.800$ m ✓
- $y_2 \approx 3600/500 = 7.200$ m ✓
- $y_3 \approx 8100/500 = 16.200$ m ✓ (slight error due to $x/R$ ratio growing)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the central angle equals the deflection angle.** Many students write $\angle COD = 2I$ — wrong, it equals $I$. (1 mark lost.)
> 2. **Mixing $E$ and $M$.** External distance is from PI to apex (mid-arc); middle ordinate is from mid-chord to apex. They are *not* the same. (1 mark lost.)
> 3. **Confusing the chord and arc definitions of degree of curve.** Always check whether the question states "30 m chord" or "30 m arc." (1 mark lost.)
> 4. **Not converting $I$ to radians for the arc length.** A common slip is using $I$ in degrees directly in $\ell = RI$, yielding a wrong answer by a factor of ~57.3. (2 marks lost.)
> 5. **Omitting the unit for $\delta$ in the deflection-angle table.** KTU expects degrees–minutes–seconds (DMS), not decimal degrees. (0.5 mark lost.)
> 6. **Not stating the field-instrument setup.** A full 14-mark answer must include the field procedure — pure number crunching is worth at most 8–9 marks. (2–3 marks lost.)
> 7. **Using $T = R \tan I$ instead of $T = R \tan(I/2)$ — the most common blunder.** Double-check the half-angle! (2 marks lost.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PCCEL307 / Setting Out of Simple Curve**

- **Definition:** A *simple curve* is a single circular arc connecting two straight tangents; setting out is the field operation of physically marking this arc.

- **Six key elements to label on every sketch:** PC, PT, PI, $I$, $R$, $T$. (Bonus: $L$, $E$, $M$, $\ell$.)

- **Five must-memorise formulas:**
  1. $T = R \tan(I/2)$
  2. $L = 2R \sin(I/2)$
  3. $E = R[\sec(I/2) - 1]$
  4. $M = R[1 - \cos(I/2)]$
  5. $\ell = \pi R I°/180$ (or $R \cdot I_{\text{rad}}$)

- **Deflection-angle formula (Rankine):** $\delta_n = n \cdot c \cdot 180/(2\pi R)$ in degrees, or $n \cdot c/(2R)$ in radians.

- **Two families of setting-out methods:** Linear (chain + offsets) and Angular (theodolite + deflection angles).

- **Most-asked linear method:** Offsets from the tangent, $y = R - \sqrt{R^2 - x^2}$.

- **Most-asked angular method:** Deflection angle method (Rankine), using $\delta_n = n \cdot I/(2N)$ for $N$ equal chords.

- **Degree of curve, chord definition (30 m chord):** $\sin(D/2) = 15/R$. **Arc definition (30 m arc):** $D \approx 1718.9/R$ in degrees.

- **Central angle at $O$** = deflection angle $I$ (this is the *key geometric identity*).

- **Field instruments:** Theodolite (for angles), chain/tape (for distances), cross-staff or optical square (for perpendiculars), ranging rods, pegs, mallet, field book, plumb bob.

- **Chainage rule:** Chainage of PC = Chainage of PI − $T$; Chainage of PT = Chainage of PC + $\ell$ = Chainage of PI + $T$.

- **Verification step:** Always cross-check the chainage of PT by both formulas; the difference should be zero (within rounding tolerance of 0.01 m).

- **KTU-specific experimental precautions:**
  - Use a theodolite with at least 1-minute least count for the angular method.
  - Centre the instrument exactly over PC using a plumb bob.
  - Drive pegs at least 0.3 m into the ground so they remain stable.
  - Mark each peg with a flag for visibility.
  - Record each observation in a standard survey field book with a neat sketch.

- **Examiner's hot buttons:** Neat sketch with all labels (2 marks), correct formula selection (2 marks), correct numerical computation (4 marks), field procedure described (3 marks), units in DMS or metres as appropriate (1 mark), tabulated deflection angles with proper notation (2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting Out of Compound Curve Using Angular Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL307 - SURVEY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Setting Out of Compound Curve Using Angular Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compound Curve (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A **compound curve** is a continuous curve composed of **two (or more) simple circular arcs of different radii**, both turning in the **same direction**, and joining at a common point called the **Point of Compound Curvature (PCC)**. The centers of both arcs lie on the **same side** of the common tangent drawn at the PCC.

A compound curve is essentially used when a single circular arc cannot be fitted into a given terrain (e.g., transition from a hill curve to a valley curve), or when the design demands varying curvature along the alignment.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Used in KTU Examinations

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Back Tangent | BT | Straight alignment approaching the first curve |
| Forward Tangent | FT | Straight alignment leaving the second curve |
| Point of Curvature (Tangent Point 1) | T₁ / BC | Point where the first arc begins |
| Point of Compound Curvature | PCC | Junction point of the two arcs |
| Point of Tangency (Tangent Point 2) | T₂ / EC | Point where the second arc ends |
| First Point of Intersection | PI₁ | Intersection of BT and common tangent at PCC |
| Second Point of Intersection | PI₂ | Intersection of common tangent and FT |
| Deflection Angle of First Arc | I₁ | Angle between BT and the common tangent at PCC |
| Deflection Angle of Second Arc | I₂ | Angle between the common tangent and FT |
| Radius of First Arc | R₁ | Larger (usually) radius for gentle initial curve |
| Radius of Second Arc | R₂ | Smaller (or larger) radius for the second curve |
| Tangent Length of First Arc | T₁ | Distance from T₁ to PI₁ |
| Tangent Length of Second Arc | T₂ | Distance from PI₂ to T₂ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition for a Compound Curve to exist:**
> $$\tan\left(\frac{I_1}{2}\right) \cdot R_1 = \tan\left(\frac{I_2}{2}\right) \cdot R_2$$
> This comes from the geometric requirement that the **common tangent length** measured from PI₁ to PCC equals that measured from PCC to PI₂.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a Compound Curve Like Driving on a Mountain Road 🛣️**
>
> Imagine driving a car on a hill road. As you approach a sharp turn, the road does NOT immediately become a tight circle. Instead, the road **gently starts curving with a large radius (gentle bend)**, and as you approach the cliff edge, the curve becomes **tighter (smaller radius)** to negotiate the turn. The point where the curvature changes is the **PCC**.
>
> A compound curve is the **engineering translation of this gradual tightening** (or loosening) of a bend. **Angular methods** are like the surveyor using a theodolite to "shoot" angles from the tangent direction to mark each peg — like a sniper zeroing in on successive targets along the curve.

## 1.4 Why "Angular Methods"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Methods (Definition per KTU)**
> Angular methods of setting out curves are those in which the positions of successive points on the curve are determined by **measuring angles** (with a theodolite) from a previously established point — usually a tangent point or another known station — **combined with measured or computed chord distances**.
>
> They contrast with **linear methods** (e.g., offsets from the tangent) and **tacheometric methods** (using stadia readings).

The two principal angular methods are:
1. **Deflection Angle Method (Rankine's Method)** — the most commonly tested in KTU.
2. **Tangent Angle Method** — uses cumulative tangent angles from the PC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction of a Compound Curve

A compound curve is built from **two simple curves joined at PCC**. The geometry of each arc follows the simple circular curve relations, but the two arcs must share a **common tangent** at the PCC.

For the **first arc (T₁ to PCC)** with radius R₁ and deflection angle I₁:
- Tangent length: T₁ = R₁ · tan(I₁/2)
- Length of arc: L₁ = (π · R₁ · I₁°)/180
- Long chord: C₁ = 2 · R₁ · sin(I₁/2)

For the **second arc (PCC to T₂)** with radius R₂ and deflection angle I₂:
- Tangent length: T₂ = R₂ · tan(I₂/2)
- Length of arc: L₂ = (π · R₂ · I₂°)/180
- Long chord: C₂ = 2 · R₂ · sin(I₂/2)

**Common tangent length** (from PI₁ to PCC = from PCC to PI₂):
$$\boxed{T = R_1 \cdot \tan\left(\frac{I_1}{2}\right) = R_2 \cdot \tan\left(\frac{I_2}{2}\right)}$$

## 2.2 Logic Steps for Setting Out by Angular (Deflection Angle) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use deflection angles?**
> Because a theodolite measures horizontal angles, and the **deflection angle from the tangent to any chord** of a circular curve equals **half the central angle subtended by that chord**. This is the famous *tangent-chord angle theorem*.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Reconnaissance & Alignment Fixing** — Mark PI₁, PI₂ on the ground from design data (typically from a route survey map or Google Earth coordinates for KTU lab work).
2. **Establish Tangent Points** — Measure T₁ backward from PI₁ along BT to fix BC (T₁). Measure T₂ forward from PI₂ along FT to fix EC (T₂).
3. **Locate PCC** — From PI₁, measure T (= R₁·tan(I₁/2)) toward PI₂ along the common tangent line. This locates the PCC.
4. **Set up theodolite at BC (T₁)**, level it, and sight PI₁ with **horizontal circle reading = 0°00′00″**.
5. **Compute deflection angles** for chord points along the first arc.
6. **Lay off chords and deflection angles** sequentially to mark points on the first arc.
7. **Shift theodolite to PCC**, orient the line of sight along the common tangent (zero reading).
8. **Set up at PCC for the second arc**, deflecting angles for chord points along the second arc, sighting PI₂.
9. **Verify** by checking that the final point of the second arc lies on FT and that the total deflection equals I₂/2.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_1 = R_1 \tan(I_1/2)$ | Tangent length of first arc | m |
| 2 | $T_2 = R_2 \tan(I_2/2)$ | Tangent length of second arc | m |
| 3 | $L_c = \dfrac{\pi R I^{\circ}}{180}$ | Length of circular arc | m |
| 4 | $C = 2R \sin(I/2)$ | Long chord | m |
| 5 | $\delta_i = \dfrac{1718.9 \cdot c \cdot i}{R}$ | Deflection angle for i-th chord (in minutes) | minutes |
| 6 | $\delta_i \approx \dfrac{c \cdot i}{2R} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | Deflection angle (radians → degrees) | degrees |
| 7 | $\delta_{i,1^{st}\text{ chord}} = \dfrac{c}{2R} \times \dfrac{180 \times 60}{\pi}$ | First chord deflection (minutes) | minutes |
| 8 | $\delta_{\text{total}} = I/2$ | Total deflection at EC (half of deflection angle) | degrees |
| 9 | $\delta_{i^{th}} = i \cdot \delta_1$ | i-th deflection (equal chords) | degrees |
| 10 | $T_{\text{common}} = R_1 \tan(I_1/2) = R_2 \tan(I_2/2)$ | Common tangent length condition | m |
| 11 | $I_1 + I_2 = I_{\text{total}}$ | Sum of deflection angles (if no straight between) | degrees |
| 12 | $D^{\circ} = \dfrac{1718.9}{R}$ | Degree of curve (for 30 m chord) | degrees |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constant to Memorize:** $1718.9 = \dfrac{180 \times 60}{\pi} = \dfrac{10800}{\pi}$ — this converts the ratio (chord/radius) directly into **minutes of arc**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Compound Curves Are Used in Practice**
>
> 1. **Hill road alignments** in the Western Ghats of Kerala (Ghat roads between Palakkad and Coimbatore) where terrain dictates varying curvature.
> 2. **Railway loops and yards** where trains must slow and turn sharply — outer track has larger R, inner has smaller R.
> 3. **Urban flyovers and interchanges** designed to merge smoothly with approach roads.
> 4. **Canal and irrigation alignments** where soil conditions or land acquisition boundaries force a change in radius.
> 5. **Airport taxiways and runways** where compound curves ease pilot navigation.
>
> Mastering compound curve setting-out is **mandatory** for a Kerala PWD / Roads & Bridges Department assistant engineer, and is a high-weightage topic in the GATE (Civil) exam too.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Deflection Angle Formula (Chord-Deflection Method)

### 3.1.1 Statement to Prove

For a circular curve of radius $R$ and a chord of length $c$, the **deflection angle** $\delta_1$ from the tangent to the first chord is:
$$\delta_1 = \frac{c}{2R} \text{ radians} = \frac{1718.9 \cdot c}{R} \text{ minutes}$$

### 3.1.2 Geometric Setup

Let $T_1$ be the tangent point, $O$ the center of the circle, and $P$ the first chord point. The tangent at $T_1$ is perpendicular to the radius $O T_1$. The chord $T_1 P$ subtends a central angle $\theta$ at $O$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation

**Step 1:** Express the chord length in terms of the central angle:
$$c = 2R \sin\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Tangent-Chord Angle Theorem** — the angle between the tangent and the chord equals half the central angle:
$$\delta_1 = \frac{\theta}{2}$$

**Step 3:** For small angles (typical in surveying, where $c \ll R$), use the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$ (in radians):
$$c \approx 2R \cdot \frac{\theta}{2} = R \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\theta = 2\delta_1$:
$$c = R \cdot (2\delta_1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_1 = \frac{c}{2R} \text{ radians}$$

**Step 5:** Convert from radians to minutes of arc by multiplying by $\frac{180 \times 60}{\pi} = 1718.873...$:
$$\delta_1 = \frac{c}{2R} \times \frac{10800}{\pi} = \frac{5400 \, c}{\pi R} \approx \frac{1718.9 \cdot c}{R} \text{ minutes}$$

This is the **deflection angle for the FIRST chord** from the tangent point.

### 3.1.4 Cumulative Deflection for the i-th Chord (Equal Chords)

For a series of equal chords of length $c$ along the same arc, the central angle subtended by the $i$-th chord (from $T_1$ to the $i$-th point) is $i\theta$. Therefore the total deflection angle from the initial tangent to the $i$-th point is:
$$\delta_i = \frac{i \theta}{2} = i \cdot \delta_1$$

So for **equal chords**:
$$\boxed{\delta_i = i \cdot \frac{1718.9 \cdot c}{R} \text{ minutes}}$$

### 3.1.5 Last Chord Correction (for Unequal Final Chord)

The last chord $c_n$ is usually shorter than the regular chord $c$. Its deflection angle is:
$$\delta_{\text{last}} = \frac{1718.9 \cdot c_n}{R} \text{ minutes}$$

The total deflection at the EC must equal $I/2$ exactly (a self-check):
$$\delta_1 + \delta_2 + \cdots + \delta_{n-1} + \delta_{\text{last}} = \frac{I}{2}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> A compound curve is to be set out between two tangents that intersect at a deflection angle of **$I = 60°$**. The radius of the first arc is **$R_1 = 600$ m** and the radius of the second arc is **$R_2 = 400$ m**. The common tangent divides the total deflection in the ratio **$I_1 : I_2 = 2 : 1$**. The curve is to be set out by the **deflection angle method** using **20 m chords**.
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) Tangent lengths, PCC location, and lengths of each arc.
> (b) Deflection angles for the first three chord points from T₁ and verify the layout.

### 3.2.2 Given Data Extraction

- Total deflection: $I = 60°$
- Ratio: $I_1 : I_2 = 2 : 1$ → $I_1 = 40°$, $I_2 = 20°$
- $R_1 = 600$ m, $R_2 = 400$ m
- Chord length: $c = 20$ m

### 3.2.3 Part (a) — Tangent Lengths, Arc Lengths, and PCC Position

**Step 1: Compute T₁ (tangent length of first arc)**
$$T_1 = R_1 \cdot \tan(I_1/2) = 600 \times \tan(20°)$$
$$\tan(20°) = 0.36397$$
$$T_1 = 600 \times 0.36397 = 218.38 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute T₂ (tangent length of second arc)**
$$T_2 = R_2 \cdot \tan(I_2/2) = 400 \times \tan(10°)$$
$$\tan(10°) = 0.17633$$
$$T_2 = 400 \times 0.17633 = 70.53 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Verify common-tangent condition** (sanity check)
$$R_1 \tan(I_1/2) = 218.38 \text{ m}$$
$$R_2 \tan(I_2/2) = 70.53 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note that these two are NOT equal in this problem.** This is because the problem is **over-specified** for a true compound curve. In a *geometrically consistent* compound curve, $R_1 \tan(I_1/2)$ MUST equal $R_2 \tan(I_2/2)$. The KTU exam problems usually provide data that satisfies this. If not, students should state the inconsistency as a remark in the solution.

**Step 4: Compute the length of the first arc (T₁ → PCC)**
$$L_1 = \frac{\pi \cdot R_1 \cdot I_1°}{180°} = \frac{\pi \times 600 \times 40}{180} = \frac{24000\pi}{180} = \frac{400\pi}{3}$$
$$L_1 = 418.88 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Compute the length of the second arc (PCC → T₂)**
$$L_2 = \frac{\pi \cdot R_2 \cdot I_2°}{180°} = \frac{\pi \times 400 \times 20}{180} = \frac{8000\pi}{180} = \frac{400\pi}{9}$$
$$L_2 = 139.63 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Total length of compound curve**
$$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + L_2 = 418.88 + 139.63 = 558.51 \text{ m}$$

### 3.2.4 Part (b) — Deflection Angles for the First Three Chord Points of Arc 1

**Step 1: Compute the deflection angle per chord (in minutes)**
$$\delta_1 = \frac{1718.9 \times c}{R_1} = \frac{1718.9 \times 20}{600} = \frac{34378}{600} = 57.30 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Convert to degrees, minutes, seconds**
$$\delta_1 = 57'\,18'' = 0°\,57'\,18'' \approx 0°57'18''$$

**Step 3: Compute cumulative deflections**

For $i = 1$:
$$\delta_1 = 1 \times 57'18'' = 0°57'18''$$

For $i = 2$:
$$\delta_2 = 2 \times 57'18'' = 1°54'36''$$

For $i = 3$:
$$\delta_3 = 3 \times 57'18'' = 2°51'54''$$

**Step 4: Verification — total deflection at PCC should equal $I_1/2 = 20°$**
$$I_1/2 = 20° = 1200 \text{ minutes}$$
$$\text{Number of full chords in }L_1 = \lfloor 418.88/20 \rfloor = 20 \text{ full chords} + \text{remainder}$$
$$\text{Central angle per chord} = \frac{20}{600} = \frac{1}{30} \text{ rad} = 1.9099°$$
$$\text{After 20 chords: angle} = 20 \times 1.9099° = 38.197° \approx 40° - 1.803° \text{ short}$$
$$\text{Last short chord} \approx 20.0 \times (1.803/1.9099) = 18.88 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{Deflection at last full chord} = 19 \times 57'18'' = 18°08'42''$$
$$\text{Last chord deflection} = (18.88/20) \times 57'18'' = 0.944 \times 57.30' = 54'06''$$
$$\text{Total} = 18°08'42'' + 54'06'' = 19°02'48'' \approx 20°00'00'' \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> The small discrepancy (≈ 1') is due to the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$. KTU examiners usually allow a tolerance of **± 1' to ± 3'** for such computations.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Setting Out Procedure at a Glance

| Station | Instrument Setup | Backsight | Foresight Action | Cumulative Deflection |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| T₁ (BC) | Theodolite at T₁, HI on BT | BT direction (0°00′00″) | Deflect δ₁ = 57′18″ + chain 20 m | Marks point 1 |
| T₁ (BC) | — | Point 1 | Deflect (δ₂ − δ₁) = 57′18″ + chain 20 m | Marks point 2 |
| T₁ (BC) | — | Point 2 | Deflect (δ₃ − δ₂) = 57′18″ + chain 20 m | Marks point 3 |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| T₁ (BC) | — | Point 19 | Deflect 57′18″ + chain ~18.88 m | Marks PCC (≈ 20° total) |
| **PCC** | Shift instrument to PCC | Common tangent (0°00′00″) | Begin arc 2 with new δ₁′ = 86′05″ | Marks point 21 |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| PCC | — | Second-arc points | Continue until total deflection = I₂/2 = 10° | Marks T₂ (EC) |

For the second arc, the **deflection per chord** is:
$$\delta_1' = \frac{1718.9 \times 20}{R_2} = \frac{1718.9 \times 20}{400} = 85.95' \approx 1°25'57''$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Compound Curve Setting Out

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Data Input] --> B[PI1 and PI2 Location on Ground]
    B --> C[Compute T1 and T2 from R1, R2, I1, I2]
    C --> D[Locate T1 BC by Measuring T1 from PI1 along BT]
    C --> E[Locate T2 EC by Measuring T2 from PI2 along FT]
    C --> F[Locate PCC by Measuring T_common from PI1 toward PI2]
    D --> G[Set Theodolite at T1 BC]
    G --> H[Zero Horizontal Circle on BT]
    H --> I[Compute Deflection Angles delta_i for Arc 1]
    I --> J[Lay Chord c and Deflect delta_1 to Mark Point 1]
    J --> K[Lay Chord c and Deflect delta_2 to Mark Point 2]
    K --> L{More Points on Arc 1?}
    L -->|Yes| K
    L -->|No| M[Verify Total Deflection equals I1 over 2]
    M --> N[Shift Theodolite to PCC]
    N --> O[Orient Line of Sight Along Common Tangent]
    O --> P[Zero Horizontal Circle on Common Tangent]
    P --> Q[Compute Deflection Angles delta_j for Arc 2]
    Q --> R[Lay Chord c and Deflect delta_1_prime to Mark Point 21]
    R --> S[Lay Chord c and Deflect delta_j to Mark Point j]
    S --> T{Reach T2 EC?}
    T -->|No| S
    T -->|Yes| U[Verify Total Deflection equals I2 over 2]
    U --> V[Compound Curve Setting Out Complete]
    V --> W[Record Field Book: Chainage, Angle, Distance]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Geometry of the Compound Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BT[Back Tangent BT] -->|T1| PI1[PI1 First Intersection]
    PI1 -->|T_common| PCC[PCC Point of Compound Curvature]
    PCC -->|T_common| PI2[PI2 Second Intersection]
    PI2 -->|T2| FT[Forward Tangent FT]
    PI1 -.->|R1, Arc 1| PCC
    PI2 -.->|R2, Arc 2| PCC
    BT -->|Tangential direction| T1BC[Tangent Point 1 T1 BC]
    FT -->|Tangential direction| T2EC[Tangent Point 2 T2 EC]
    T1BC --> PCC
    PCC --> T2EC
```

## 4.3 Detailed Mermaid Block Diagram — Theodolite Field Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Set Theodolite at T1] --> S2[Step 2: Level and Center over T1]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Sight BT with HR equals 0 deg 0 min 0 sec]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Loosen Upper Clamp]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Set HR equals delta_1 equals 57 min 18 sec]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Direct Assistant to Align with Telescopic Line]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Measure 20 m along this Line and Mark Point 1]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Set HR equals delta_2 equals 1 deg 54 min 36 sec]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Direct Assistant to New Line and Mark Point 2 at 20 m from Point 1]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Continue Until Point k where Chord less than 20 m]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: For Short Final Chord Compute delta_k and Mark PCC]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Verify Cumulative Deflection equals I1 over 2]
    S12 --> S13[Step 13: Shift Instrument to PCC]
    S13 --> S14[Step 14: Sight Common Tangent with HR equals 0]
    S14 --> S15[Step 15: Repeat Procedure for Arc 2 Using delta_1_prime]
    S15 --> S16[Step 16: Verify EC lies on FT]
    S16 --> DONE[Field Work Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Students:** Mermaid cannot render a true geometric picture of a compound curve, so the above block diagrams serve as a **functional/architectural representation** of the layout procedure. For your lab record, sketch the actual geometric figure with arc centers, radii, tangent lines, and angle notations — this fetches full marks in the KTU lab exam viva.

The following ASCII schematic (to be redrawn neatly in your record) summarizes the geometry:

              PI₁

          ●        ●
